Bristol, 1885, to Dec., 1905; visited Straits Settlements as umpire in Tanjong Pagar Dock Arbitration, 1905-6.
II, 1960–61; retd., aptd. asst. advsr., Aden, 1966.

STANNUS, HUGH STANNUS.—M.B. (Lond.), M.R.C.S. (Eng.), L.R.C.P. (Lond.), 1901; M.D., Lond. Univ. in Trop. med., 1911; D.T.M. and H., Cantab., 1912; univ. gold medal in tropical medicine; med. offr., Nyasaland Prot., May, 1905; sen. med. offr. (with temp. rank of major), Nyasaland Field Force, Aug., 1914; sen. med. offr. to forces in Nyasaland Prot., Aug., 1915; deputy dir. of med. services, Mar., 1916.

SRESHTA, MAURICE SALVADOR.—B. 1873; B.A., Madras; Barrister-at-law; cadet, Ceylon civ. ser., Oct., 1896; off. asst. to govt. agt., E. Prov., Jan., 1898; Sabaragamuwa, Dec., 1898; addl. pol. mag., Tangalla, Sept., 1900; pol. mag., Panadure, Dec., 1900; Avisawella, Dec., 1901; Galle, Sept., 1905; comsnr. of requests, Colombo, Nov., 1907; dist. judge, Jaffna, Jan., 1911; ditto, Negombo, Nov., 1915.

STANTON, CAMERON.—B. 1861; 3rd cls. clk., dept. of finance, Canada, 1879; promoted to 2nd cls., 1883; transfd. to dept. of marine and fisheries, 1888; promoted to 1st cls., 1892; ch. clerkship, 1901; asst. dep. min., marine and fisheries, 1908.

SPENCER, CYRIL EDWARD.—B. 1873; entd. col. ser., Cyprus, 1st Mar., 1891; in secretariat from Apr., 1892, to May, 1898; also clk. to legis. coun., Mar., 1894, to May, 1898; priv. sec. to high comsnr. on several occasions, 1898-1900; inspr., mil. pol., 1st May, 1898; ag. dist. comdt., July, 1900, to Jan., 1904; apptd. in comdt. of divs. of pol., Jan., 1904; also gov. of pris. and asst. to dist. comsnr., and dep. coroner; ag. dist. comsnr., Apr. to Dec., 1905; passed in mod. Greek, higher standard, 1904; and in Turkish, lower standard, 1906; transfd. to B. East Africa, as asst. dist. comsnr., Nov., 1906; ag. supt., inland rev., Apr. to Oct., 1907; ag. dist. comsnr., Mombasa, from July, 1907; dist. comsnr., E.A.P., Apr., 1912.

ST. AUBYN, GEOFFREY PETER.—Called to the bar, Mid. Tem., Apr., 1880; W. circuit, pol. mag., Kingstown, St. Vincent, 1891; mem. legis. coun., 1891; ag. ch. just., June, 1893; ag. treas., Nov., 1893; ag. atty.-gen., Dec. to May, 1894, and Nov., 1895; ag. ch. just., from Nov., 1896, to July, 1898; judge of Turks and Caicos Islds., July, 1898; ag. comsnr., 1899, 1900, 1901, 1903, 1904, 1905 and 1906.

STANLEY, HERBERT JAMES, C.M.G. (1913).—B. 1872; ed. at Eton and Balliol Coll., Oxford (B.A.); priv. sec. to H.M. min. resident at Dresden and Coburg, and British vice-consul at Dresden, 1897-1902; asst. priv. sec. to first Lord of the Admiralty, 1906-1908; priv. sec. to Lord Pres. of the Coun., 1908-1910; priv. sec. to Viscount Gladstone, gov. gen. of Union of S. Africa, 1910; sec. to gov.-gen., 1913; resident comsnr., Southern and Northern Rhodesia, 1st Apl., 1915.

UNIACKE, CAPT. ANDREW GORE, D.S.O.—Served with N.W. mtd. pol., Canada (ex Yukon expedn.), 1893-1898; served in S. Afr. war, with Canadian forces and M.I., 1899 (2 medals, 8 clasps, D.S.O.); dist. sup. Nigeria police, 1903; served with force against Okpotos, 1903-4 (A.G.S. medal and bar); served with Nigeria Regt., 1916-18; inspr. N. Provs., Nigeria pol., 1919.

SPROULE, PERCY JULIAN, B.A., Camb.—B. 1873; barrister-at-law (Mid. Tem.); cadet, S.S., Nov., 1896; ag. dep. regisar, sup. ct., Penang, Mar., 1897; passed final in Malay, Mar., 1899; ag. 3rd mag., Penang, June, 1899; dep. regisar, sup. ct., Penang, Sept., 1899; ag. asst. regisar, sup. ct., Penang, May, 1903; ag. 2nd mag., Sing., Sept., 1904; ag. dep. pub. pros., May, 1905; ag. sol.-gen., Penang, May-Aug., 1906; dep. pub. prosecutor, Sing., Aug., 1906; ag. solr.-gen., Penang, Mar., 1908; ag. regisar, supreme ct., Penang, Dec., 1908; ag. solr.-gen., July, 1909; solr.-gen., Apl., 1911; ag. judcl. consnr., F.M.S., Jan., 1912.

BACH. (1909).—B. 1862; ed. at S. African Coll., Cape Univ. and Downing Coll., Cambridge; obtained scholarship at Middle Temp.; joined Cape bar, 1886, and Transvaal bar in 1887; legal adviser to Lord Roberts, 1900-1901; puisne judge of sup. ct., Transvaal, Mar., 1902; puisne judge of sup. ct. of S. Africa (Transvaal Provl. Div.), May, 1910; judge pres., 14th June, 1920.

SENATOR FREDERICK LIGORI, K.C., L.L.D.—B. 1845; ed. Marieville Coll.; called to Quebec bar, 1868; K.C. (Que.), 1885; K.O. (Dom.), 1889; mem. of R. comsn.in Whelan case, 1890; called to Senate, 1902; P.C. (Canada), Sept., 1925. BÉLAND, HON.

RGYLE, SIR STANLEY SEYMOUR, K.B.E. (30), M.B., B.S., M.R.C.S. (Eng.), L.R.C.P. (Ed.)—B. 1867; ed. Trinity Coll., Melbourne Univ.; lieut.-col., A.A.M.C., A.I.F. in European war; M.L.A., Victoria since 1920; ch. sec. four ministries, 1923-29; premier and treas. of Victoria, May, 1932-Apr., 1935; leader of opposition since Apr., 1935.

ABDUL MALEK BIN YUSUF.—B. 1899; ed. Malay Coll., K. Kangsar; probr., Malay Coll., K. Kangsar, 1914; Malay offr., grade III, June, 1917; do., grade II, June, 1920; offr., cls. V, M.C.S., Mar. 1930; ag. dist. offr., Krian, July, 1933; offr. cls. IV., Mar., 1934; 2nd mag., Kuala Lumpur, Nov., 1938; offr., cls. III, Mar., 1939.

AMES, CECIL GERAINT.—B. 1897; ed. Elstree Prep. Schl. and Dover Coll.; (schol.); 5th Somerset Light Infy., 1915-19; Law Soc.'s final exam. (hons.), 1921; Nigerian admstve. serv., 1922; ag. ch. registr., sup. ct., Nov. 1932–July, 1933; admitted, solr., Nov., 1933; attd. judl. dept., Jan.–Mar., 1934; asst. judge, high ct., Apr., 1934.

STANLEY, W. B.—Served with 1st Border regt., occupation of Crete 1898; S. Africa, 1899-1900 (medal and 4 clasps); 2nd lieut., W. India regt., 1900; lieut., 1901; ag. adjut., 3rd W. India regt., Gambia expedn., 1901, (medal and clasp); ag. trav. comsnr., Gambia, May and June, 1901; trav. comsnr., Gambia, Aug., 1901; A.D.C. and priv. sec. to ag. gov., Gambia, Aug. to Oct., 1901; capt., 1902; passed course of survey, schl. of mil. engineering, Chatham, 1905; resig. comsn., 1906; capt., 3rd Yorks regt., 1906; qualified in native language; dist. comsnr., S. Leone, 1910.

STANFORD, HON. WALTER ERNEST MORTIMER, C.B. (1901), C.M.G. (1892).—B. 1850; mag.'s clk., Cape Col., 1863; mag., 1876; capt. of levies in Gcaleka war, 1877-8 (medal); mem. native laws and customs comsn., 1880-2; comdt. in war of 1880-1; on special service to Pondoland, 1884; ch. mag. Griqualand E., 1885; negotiated treaty with Pondos, 1886; sec. nat. affairs dept., 1898; ditto and ch. mag., July, 1904; mem. of S. African native affairs comsn., Sept., 1903, to Jan., 1905; holds rank of colonel in Cape colonial forces; ret., May, 1907; M.L.A. for Tembuland, 1908; mem. of S. African National Convention in connection with South Africa Bill, 1909. STANLEY, HON. SIR ARTHUR LYULPH, K.C.M.G. (1914).—B. 1875; ed. Oxford Univ. (B.A.); called to the bar, Inner Temple, 1902; M.P. for Eddisbury div. of Cheshire, 1906-10; gov. of Victoria, 12th Jan., 1914; assumed govt., 23rd Feb., 1914.

AMERY, WILLIAM BANKES, C.B.E. (1920).—B. 1883; ed. Christ's Hosp.; ent. cust. and excise dept., 1901; asst. sec. (temp.) war trade dept., 1916; stab. offr., ministry of transport, 1919; finance offr. (with rank of prin.) overseas settlement dept., D.O., 1922; rep. of the U.K. govt. in the Commonwealth of Australia under the Empire Settlement Act, 1925; prin., O.S.D., 1929; temp. attd., D.O., 1932.

AMERASINGHE, SAMSON FELIX, B.A., antab.—B. 1902; cadet, Ceylon civ. serv., July, 26; attd., Kalutara kach., Aug., 1926; office st. govt. agt., E. Prov., Dec., 1927; pol. mag. andagamuwa, Sept., 1928; ditto, Negombo, Dec., 28; office asst. to govt. agt., Uva, June, 30; office asst. to govt. agt., N.W. Prov., June, 32; pol. mag., Kandy, Nov., 1932; addnl. asst. vt. agt., Colombo, Oct., 1935.

CAHAN. HON. CHARLES HAZLITT, B.A., LL.B., LL.D., K.C.—B. 1861; ed. Yarmouth Seminary and Dalhousie Univ.; leader of conserv. party in Nova Scotia legis., 1890-94; 1st el. to H. of C., Canada at g.e., 1925; re-el., 1926, 1930 and 1935; sec. of state in Bennett cabinet, 1930.

AMBLE, CAPT. GOODMAN, M.C., V.D., R.G.S.—B. 1885; ed. Steyning Grammar Schl., Iighton Grammar Schl. and St. Mark's II., Chelsea; asst. mast., Victoria Inst., Lumpur, F.M.S., Jan., 1910; war serv.,ance and Gallipoli, 1914-19; rejoined Victoria st., 1919; sec., Selangor cadets with rank of pt., Mar., 1924; Col. Aux. Forces Long Serv. ed., Mar., 1924; headmast., Outram schl., bore, Apr., 1926; lieut., S.V.C., May, 1926; pt., Aug., 1930; transfd. to S.S.V.F., R. of O.; nr. normal instr., S'pore, Dec., 1931; headmast., Itram schl., Nov., 1932.

ABNER, CHARLES J.—B. 1876; on mily. , France and Flanders, Sept., 1914 to Mar., 9; clk., Army pay office, R. Engurs., Mar. to ., 1919; clk., Army pay office, command mr., Overseas Bases, Oct., 1919 to July, 1922; offr., C.O., 10th July, 1922.

AMBLER, LLOYD EDWARD.—B. 1894; clk., stal dept., Fiji, June, 1909; 4th cls. clk., ids dept., Aug., 1913; served with 1st Batt., R.R.C., France, 1916-17; Indian Army Res. comsnd., Oct., 1917; Baluchistan, 1917-19; 1 cls. clk., col. sec.'s office, Nov., 1916; td., lands dept., Fiji, Sept., 1919; ag. 2nd clk. and sec., mining bd., Oct., 1919-Oct., 20; ag. sub-inspr. constab. (during Indian ike), Feb., 1920; ag. 1st cls. clk. and clk., exec. d leg. couns., Jan. to Apr., 1921; regy. clk., estern Pacific high comsn., May, 1921; ag. ch. r., Sept., 1922 to Feb., 1923; 1st cls. clk., Jan., 27; ag. ch. clk. and acctnt., on various occa-sns., 1923-31; ag. sec., Western Pacific high msn., June to Aug., 1931; ch. clk. and acct., n., 1932.

AMERY, LIEUT.-COL. L.C.M.S.—B. 1873; at Harrow and Balliol Coll., Oxford; late fellow of All Souls, Oxford; Ouseley Scholar Imp. Inst. in Turkish; called to the bar, Inner Temple, 1902; on editorial staff of The Times , 1899-1909; served during the war in Flanders, Monika, and the Near East; on the staff of theopl. War Coun. at Versailles, and as asst. sec. the War Cabinet and Impl. Cabinet to Jan., 1919; under-sec. of state for the colonies, 14th Jan., 1919; acted as sec. of state during Lord Milner's absence in Egypt, Nov., 1919 to Jan., 1920; First Lord of the Admty., Oct., 1922; S. of S. for the Cols., 7th Nov., 1924; also of S. for Dominion affrs., 9th July, 1925 to June, 1929; capt. 14th 1st B'ham Batt., Royal Warwickshire Regt., 1914; lieut.-col. on general staff, 1917; M.P. (U) for S. Birmingham, May, 1911; ditto Sparkbrook div., Dec., 1918; edited "The Times History of the South African War"; author of "Problem of the Army," 1903; "Fundamental Fallacies of Free Trade," 1906; "The Great Question" (with J. M. Robertson, L.P.), 1909; "Union and Strength," 1912.

DALTON, ALFRED, A.M.Inst.T.—B. 1892; G.S. and W.R., Ireland, 1909; asst. traffic supt., Tanganyika rlys., Nov., 1920; traffic supt., 1930; asst. supt., line, Kenya, and Uganda rlys. and harbrs., 1936.

UTHBERT, MORTON.—Ed. Edin. Institn. and sgow and West of Scot. Tech. Coll.; apprent. ir. to Messrs. Dick, Kerr and Co., Ltd., Lon- 1895; electrn. with Glasgow corptn. elec. t., 1899; asst. engnr. Govan corptn. elec. dept., chief asst. engnr., Oban corptn. elec. dept., electrl. engnr., B. Honduras, 1905; exec. electrl. engnr., P.W.D., B. Honduras, 1909; t., B.H.T.F., 1917; ag. adjutant, B.H.T.F., 1917 to Jan., 1918; hon. A.D.C. to gov., B. Honduras, 1919. UTTERIDGE, JOSEPH OLIVER, M.B.E.

HENRI SEVERIN.—B. 1869; ed. Three Rivers (Que.) Coll. (B.A.), Laval Univ. (M.D.); physician; mayor of St. Joseph, 1910; el. to leg. ass., Quebec, 1897 and 1900; el. to H.C. bye-el., 1902; re-el., 1904, 1908, 1911, 1917 and 1921; postmr.-gen. in Sir Wilfrid Laurier's govt., Aug. 19th until resignation of govt., Oct. 6th, 1911; served with the Belgian forces in the early part of the war, and made prisoner of war; min. of soldiers' civ. re-est. and health in King admn., Dec., 1921; called to senate, 1925.

DALLARD, BERKELEY LIONEL.—B. 1889; . Rangiora High Schl. and Victoria Univ. Coll., Z.; ent. N.Z. govt. serv., 1907; stamp duties dept., 1907-13; audit office, 1913-18; advisory acctnt., bd. of trade, 1919-23; inspr., pub. serv. comsnr.'s office, 1924-26; contr.-gen., prisons from 1926; also asst. pub. serv. comsnr., 1929-33; and under sec. justice and registr.-gen., births, deaths and marriages, 1933.

HENRI SEVERIN.—B. 1868; ed. Three Rivers (Que.) Coll. (B.A.), Laval Univ. (M.D.); physician; mayor of St. Joseph, 1910; el. to leg. ass., Quebec, 1897 and 1900; el. H.C. bye-el., 1902; re-el., 1904, 1908, 1911, 1915 and 1921; postmr.-gen. in Sir Wilfrid Laurier govt., Aug. 19th until resignation of govt., Oct. 6th, 1911; served with the Belgian forces in the cam part of the war, and made prisoner of war; dir. of soldiers' civ. re-est. and health in Can. adminstn., Dec., 1921; called to senate, 1925.

DOUGLAS, HUGH ALASTAIR, B.A. (Forestry). —B. 1915; ed. Trinity Coll., Glenalmond and Worcester Coll., Oxford; asst. conserv., forests, Gold Coast, Jan., 1938. DOUGLAS-JONES, SIR CRAWFORD DOUGLAS, Kt. BACH.

UZEN, ALLAN LECKIE.—Served in R.E., 1-2; S. African War (medal and five clasps); se police, 1902-6; Bech. Prot. police, 1906-14; sed Cape civ. serv. lower law exam., 1914; clk. to asst. comsnr., N. Dist., Bech. Prot., 4; ag. asst. res. mag., ditto, 1915; passed 2nd le Secwana exam., 1916; 1st grade (B), ditto, 7; ag. res. mag., 1917; ag. asst. res. mag., 9; res. mag., 1921.

USSEN, RICHARD CECIL.—B. 1887; B.A., lin; barrister-at-law, Gray's Inn; cadet, S.S., 1911; ag. dist. offr. Jelebu, Oct., 1912; ed cadet, June, 1914; offr. cls. V, Dec., 1914; asst. dist. offr., B. Pahang, Feb., 1915; cls. IV, Jan. 1919; mag., Seremban, July, 1919; offr., cls. III, D.P.P., Perak, Nov., 1923; cls. II, Nov., 1928; dep. legal advr., F.M.S., cls. IB, Apr., 1929; ag. legal advr., F.M.S., 1934; ag. judge, F.M.S., Feb., 1935; ne Judge, S.S., 1936.

DALLEY, JOHN DOUGLAS.—B. 1900; pol. probr., F.M.S., Nov., 1920, probry. a.c.p., Jan., 1922; on depn. to Kedah; a.c.p., Nov., 1923; 1st a.c.p., Selangor, Dec., 1924; c.p.o., Johore S., Apr., 1926; a.c.p., K. Kangsar, June, 1931; a.s.p., S'pore, Sept., 1932; comdt., pol. depot, Nov., 1933; dep. comsnr., pol., F.M.S., Aug., 1934.

SIR CECIL HAMILTON, K.B.E. (1926), C.M.G. (1911), D.S.O. (1901).—Asst. inspr., G. Coast constab., 1894; served in Ashanti exped., 1895-6 (star); Northern Territories (Neutral Zone), 1897 (medal and clasp); inspr., 1898; priv. sec. to gov. of G. Coast and clk. of leg. and exec. couns., 1899- 1900; Ashanti campaign. 1900 (ag. res. during siege of Coomassie, D.S.O., medal and clasp); trav. comsnr., 1901; comsnr. of S. Province of Ashanti, July, 1901; comdt. of Ashanti Mines volunteers with rank of major, Nov., 1905; ag. chief comsnr. of Ashanti in 1901, 1906, 1907, 1908 and 1909; chief comsnr. of Northern Territories, Jan., 1910; gov., Gambia, 20th Nov., 1920; assumed govt., 3rd Jan., 1921.
DOUGLAS, CHARLES HOMER, M.M.—B. 1884; ed. Wellingborough Grammar Schl.; war serv., R.E., 1915-19; 2nd cls. survr., P.W.D., Hong Kong, 1921; land survr., 1933.

AKERS, COLIN HUGH, M.C.—B. 1897; ed. nch. Gram. Schl.; war serv., 1915-19; cadet, I.S., Nov., 1920; offr., cls. V, Nov., 1923; pr., liquor shops, F.M.S., Sept., 1925; 2nd t. dir., educn. (Chinese), S.S., Apr., 1926; ., cls. IV, Nov., 1927; offr., cls. III, Nov., 32; asst. dir., educn., Chinese schls., F.M.S., c., 1932; ag. prot., Chinese, Selangor and hang, Apr., 1933; do., Penang, Aug., 1934; . Selangor and Penang, May, 1935. JALE, WILLIAM LEONARD.—B. 1906; ed. mers Coll., Hull; L.L.B. Lond.; Holt. schol., ay's Inn, 1929; called to bar, 1932; N. Eastern cult, N.R.Y. and E.R.Y. sessions; legal asst.. O. and C.O., Feb., 1935; author of "Law of e Parish Church" (1933).

(1932), P., F.R.G.S.—B. 1887; ed. Borough Road , Isleworth, London, 1906-08; teacher's certif. h 4 distinctions) Bd. of Educn., Lond.; A.C.T. , Bd. of Educn., Lond.; staff-capt., H.M. ces; inspr. Q.M.G. services; maths. and draw- master, Junr. Tech. Schl., Liverpool, 1919; prin., t. training coll., Trinidad, Jan., 1921; mem., er-Col. Educn. Confce, 1921; mem. bd. ist training; supt., tech. classes, 1922; ag. r. inspr. schls., Feb., 1923; senr. inspr. schls., nidad, 1st July, 1923; ag. dir., educn., July, 5 to Mar., 1926; asst. dir., educn. and senr. or., schls., June, 1926; asst. dir., educn. and inspr., schls., Jan., 1928; ag. dir., educn., v to Nov., 1929; dir., educn., 1934; author est Indian Readers " etc.

DOUGLAS, CUTHBERT SCHOLTO.—B. 1887; temp. survr., irrign. dept., Union of S.A., May, 1917; temp. asst. engnr., Dec., 1919; 2nd gr. prof. asst., Dec., 1927; prof. asst., Nov., 1929; survr.-gen., Bloemfontein, May, 1934; do., Natal, Dec., 1937.
DOWNES, CAPT. ROGER MEADEN, M.C.— B. 1892; ed. pvte. schl. and Oxford Univ.; admstve. offr., cls. IV, Nigeria, 1921; cls. III, 1929; ag. res., Sept., 1932 and Aug.-Oct., 1936; admstve. offr., cls.

DANDURAND, HON. SENATOR RAOUL, K.C., LL.D., P.C.—B. 1861; ed. Montreal Coll. and Laval Univ.; LL.B., 1882; LL.D., 1909 and hon. LL.D., McGill, Toronto, and Queen's Univ.; pres., Univ. of Montreal, Feb., 1934; called to Quebec bar, 1883; K.C., 1898; called to senate of Canada by Lord Aberdeen, 1898; speaker of senate, 1905-9; P.C., 1909; min. of state and leader of King govt. in senate, 1921, also in 2nd King govt., 1926, and in 3rd King govt., 1935; leader of lib. party, senate, 1930; served as R. comsnr. on educn., Quebec, 1910; el. pres., 6th assem., L. of N., Geneva, 1925; del. of Canada to coun., L. of N., 1927-30; apptd. by pres. of U.S.A., arbitr. under treaty of peace with Brazil.

DALLIN, THOMAS.—Late lieut. 3rd Batt. Essex Regt.; served in S. African War (medal and two clasps); hon. 2nd lieut. in the army, Oct., 1902; joined col. audit dept., Feb., 1904; examr., exchequer and audit dept., July, 1906; asst. audr., S. Nigeria, Oct., 1906 to Feb., 1910; reverted to ex. and audit dept., Feb., 1910; asst. audr., Hong Kong, July, 1910; ag. audr. in 1911, 1912, 1913 and 1914; asst. cable censor, Aug.-Oct., 1914; on mil. serv., Mar., 1915 to July, 1916; ag. audr., Aug., 1916 to Dec., 1917; J.P., 1918; attd. to finan. branch, W.O., Sept., 1918 to Mar., 1919; ag. audr. on various occasions in 1920, 1922 and 1924-30.
II, 1957–62; retd. apptd.; admin. offr., Gam., 1962. (Gambia Govt. service.)

DOWBIGGIN, SIR HERBERT LAYARD Kt. Bach., C.M.G. (1926).—B. 1880; inspr. of pol., Ceylon, Jan., 1901; asst. supt. of pol., Apr., 1901; supt. of pol., Feb., 1905; inspr.-gen. of pol., Nov., 1913; on sp. duty, Cyprus, Nov. 1926; resumed duties as inspr.-gen., pol., Ceylon, Nov., 1926; ret., 1937.

DRAYTON, BERNARD MORGAN.—B. 1886; service in post office, pub. wks. and treasy. depts., Grenada, 1902-14; treasy. asst., 3rd grade, Nigeria, 1914; asst. treas., civ. admstrn., German E. Africa (Occupied Territory), 1917; asst. treas., Nigeria, 1920; senr. asst. treas., Nigeria, 1923. DRAYTON, HON. SIR HENRY L., Kt. BACH.

DRAKE, THOMAS NORMAN.—B. 1896; ed. Campbell Coll., Belfast; pilot, R.A.F., Great War, 1917-19; cadet, R.I.C. and dist. inspr., Ulster spl. constaby., 1922; Air Force Offrs. Reserve, 1923; 3rd cls. inspr., Jamaica constaby., 1924.

DOYLE, THOMAS FRANCIS.—B. 1891; ed. St. Patrick's Acad., Dunganon; offr. in Imp. cust. and excise serv., Aug., 1912; inspr., cust., Palestine, July, 1926; ag. dep. dir., cust., excise and trade, periods in 1929 and 1930; dep. collr., cust. and excise comsnr., Mauritius, Feb., 1932; ag. collr., cust. and harbmr., June, 1932; collr., cust. and harbr.-mast., Aug., 1936.

DRENNAN, JOHN MATTHEW, D.C.M. (1918). —B. 1891; ed. Geo. Watson's Coll., Edin.; apptd., after compet. exam., clk., 2nd div., civ. serv. and assigned to B. of T., 1910; apptd., after compet. exam., clk., 3rd cls., Crown Agents' office, Feb., 1912; on mil. serv., 1914-19 (France, adjutant, Behar Light Horse Volunteers, India, Apr., 1892 to Apr., 1897; 1st and 5th Dragoon Guards and Imperial Light Horse, Apr., 1897 to Oct., 1900; Hazara (N.-W. Frontier, India), 1888, asst. prov. marshal (ment. in desp., medal and clasp); commanded Imperial Light Horse, S. PRODUCTION AND TRADE—Unless otherwise stated, the values of imports and exports for 1950 exclude values in respect of bullion (except when exported as a commodity by a producing territory), specie, and currency notes. See pages 58–62 of The Colonial Office List 1951. The Aden College is under construction but the total cost (including provision for sports fields and ground lay-out) is now estimated to be Rs.26,22,000, a large increase over the original estimate. It has now been decided to sanction the additional expenditure on the scheme. The Technical College opened with two classes in January, 1951. The British Council no longer maintains Institutes for men and women in the Colony. The Labour and Welfare Department in Aden commenced in a small way in 1950, under the District Commissioner. In April, 1951, it became a separate department. The small staff of a Labour and Welfare Officer, an Assistant Welfare Officer, a Labour Inspector and one clerk, are busily engaged in trying to implement the Ordinances regulating labour, and in particular in endeavouring to create better labour relations. The Labour Officer is also Factory Inspector for the Colony. Recently improved labour legislation includes the compulsory reporting of industrial accidents, and the introduction for the first time in Aden of the 48-hour week for manual workers. A re-organised and active Labour Advisory Board has been created, with representatives of employers and non-employers. As there are no Trade Unions in Aden, selection of representatives for wage earners presents a problem. In social welfare the main efforts have been directed towards the children. Probation for juvenile offenders who have homes in Aden has met with marked success. The juvenile offender from the Yemen or the Protectorate who has made his way alone into the Colony presents an intractable problem. The first Play Centre for children under 12 was opened in May, 1951, and the first Boys' Clubs started early in that year. Both these latter two activities are organised by voluntary societies. A Social Welfare Advisory Council has been established, which meets regularly. A committee has investigated the problem of the blind and reported to Government its recommendations. A pontoon is now available at the Port to facilitate landing of passengers from big passenger liners. The landing-ground, Air Traffic Control Centre and Air-Sea Rescue Services are managed by the Royal Air Force. Since 1st September, 1950, a Civil Enclave has been established and a terminal building which is under construction, is expected to be completed by the end of 1951. The parking apron is also being extended to meet increasing traffic. There are no internal colony services. Aden Airways, a subsidiary of B.O.A.C., operates scheduled services to Cairo, Nairobi, Addis Ababa and Sharjah and intends to introduce a service to Karachi. Services to the Aden Protectorate are :— (1) A weekly service to Mukeiras on the Audhali plateau, with frequent additional charter flights. (2) A weekly service to Riyan and Qatn in the Hadhramaut. (3) Charter flights to Beihan. Other operators are :— Ethiopian Air Lines who have daily schedules connecting Addis Ababa and Aden via Dire-Dawa and Djibouti ; Air India International who operate Constellation aircraft on a weekly service between Bombay and Nairobi with stops at Karachi and Aden ; Misair who have recently been granted traffic rights and operate with Vikings :— (1) One service a week from Cairo via Khartoum and Asmara, and (2) One service via Djeddah and Asmara ; and Yemen Air Lines who make frequent non-scheduled flights between Taiz and Aden. Approximately three hundred aircraft a month land at or take off from Khormaksar airport carrying an average of 1,600 passengers and 146,450 kgs. of freight. (See Note on page 27.) In 1950–51 the Revenue amounted to Rs. 170,00,000 and the Expenditure to Rs.142,00,000. The main heads of taxation in 1949–50 were :— The main heads of estimated expenditure for 1951–52 are as follows :— The Colony has no public debt. For the principal rates of duty, reference should be made to the list on page 62 of The Colonial Office List 1951 amended as follows : the rates in respect of cigarettes are now Rs. 2.8.0. to Rs. 7.4.0. per thousand, according to their selling price, and those on manufactured tobacco, Rs. 1.4.0. to Rs. 2.8.0. per lb., according to its selling price. From 1st November, 1951, the currency was changed from the Indian Rupee to the East African Shilling. The Eastern Bank established an office in Crater in April, 1951. The export of salt in 1950 amounted to 251,541 tons. The tonnage produced during the year was 255,865 tons. In 1950 imports amounted to Rs.55,78,42,907 and exports to Rs.25,80,32,655. The figures for 1949 were Rs.43,67,11,407 and Rs.19,47,95,051 respectively. N.B. The above figures include re-exports but exclude bullion and specie. See pages 62–67 of The Colonial Office List 1951. The main chiefs in the Eastern Aden Protectorate, who are in protective treaty relations with Her Majesty's Government are :— The Qu'aiti State—His Highness the Sultan Sir Salih bin Ghalib Al Qu'aiti, K.C.M.G. The Kathiri State—Sultan Hussein bin Ali bin Mansur Al Kathiri. The Wahidi Sultanate of Balhaf—Sultan Nasr bin Abdullah Al Wahidi. The Wahidi Sultanate of Bir Ali—Sultan Alwi bin Salih Al Wahidi. The Mahri Sultanate of Qishn and Socotra—Sultan Ahmed bin Abdullah bin Essa bin Afrar. A similar list of chiefs in the Western Aden Protectorate will be found on page 63 of The Colonial Office List 1951. A treaty with His Majesty's Government was signed in April, 1949, by the Wahidi Sultan of Balhaf (E.A.P.) whereby the Sultan undertook to accept the advice of the Governor of Aden in all matters connected with the welfare and development of the territory of the Sultanate. In the Western Aden Protectorate the Abdali and Fadhli States have their own departments of education. The following are the 1951–52 estimated figures of Revenue and Expenditure for some of the Western Aden Protectorate States :— The following are the 1950–51 estimated figures of Revenue and Expenditure for some of the Eastern Aden Protectorate States :— The principal sources of revenue in the Eastern Aden Protectorate are customs, licences and excise duties. In the Qu'aiti State over three lakhs are spent on the Armed Constabulary and police, nearly two lakhs on medical and public health, and nearly three lakhs on education. The State has also spent large sums from reserves for agricultural improvement, irrigation, building and repairing of dams and deflectors, etc. The Kathiri State has also contributed substantially towards agricultural development. The main source of revenue in the States of the Western Aden Protectorate is customs. In the Western Aden Protectorate the Rupee was replaced by the East African Shilling, with effect from the 1st November, 1951. The Riyal (or Maria Theresa Dollar) is still used, its value fluctuating with the value of silver. In the Eastern Aden Protectorate, up to 1944, the Maria Theresa Dollar was the currency used throughout the interior, but in that year the Rupee currency was finally adopted. The Rupee has been replaced by the East African Shilling with effect from the 1st November, 1951, but the Maria Theresa Dollars remain the normal form of currency among the Bedouins, and in the less developed areas. There are no banking facilities in the Aden Protectorate. The control of agriculture and the development of the irrigation potential of the Abyan area of the Western Aden Protectorate is in the hands of a Board consisting of two members from the Lower Yafai State, two members from the Fadhli State and three officers appointed by the Governor. It is anticipated that the 1950–51 season will yield a crop of 6,000 bales of cotton. In the Eastern Aden Protectorate the main industries are agriculture, weaving, dyeing, fishing, dhow building, lime burning, and sheep and camel breeding. The staple crops are wheat, millet, dhurra, dukhn, museibli, some barley, tobacco, simsim, lucerne and some dates. There are some large plantations of banana, papau, etc., to be found in the coastal areas. Honey is found in small quantities. A new fish canning factory worked on modern lines with trawlers and motor boats is now functioning. The Governor. The Chief Secretary. The Attorney General. The Financial Secretary. E. Delete the first two sentences under this heading on page 68 of The Colonial Office List 1951 and insert :— “ San Salvador (Watling’s Island), so-called by Columbus when he made his landfall there in October, 1492, was known as Guanahani by the aboriginal Lucayan Indians and is one of the most easterly of the Bahamas. A few years after Columbus discovered the islands, the Lucayans were transported to work the mines in Hispaniola ”. To the list of institutions which provide higher education, “ St. John’s College ” should be added. Broadcast Lessons. The teacher-training lectures and adult education programmes are now broadcast in the afternoons from 3.15 p.m. to 3.45 p.m. instead of in the evenings. The North American Regional Broadcasting Conference was duly held in Canada in 1949 and continued in the United States of America in 1950. It was agreed at this Conference that the Bahamas should retain its Class 1-A clear channel on the frequency of 1,540 kcs. In August, 1950, the Bahamas station ZNS started commercial operation and increased its hours to 15 per day. New Providence is served by an adequate road system maintained by the Public Works Department. All the main roads have been surfaced with asphalt, many of them being reconditioned by the R.A.F. during the War. In the Out Islands, Eleuthera has now a good main arterial road, asphalt-surfaced over most of its length, but in the other islands the roads are mainly rough farm tracks, except in the principal towns and settlements, where they are asphalt or concrete-surfaced. Maintenance in the Out Islands is carried out by the local Board of Works. Ships of the Canadian National Steamship Line now provide a regular freight service from Halifax or Montreal. Freight service between the United Kingdom and the Colony is maintained by steamers of the Royal Mail Line and the Pacific Steam Navigation Company. The latter Line also provides a passenger service by the “ Reina del Pacifico ”. There is also a regular freight service between Nassau and New York and Florida. During the winter months, a number of cruise ships call at Nassau. Oakes’ Airport, the main airport in the Bahamas, provides 24-hour service for long and short range international and local airlines. During 1950 a considerable increase in air traffic over previous years was evident and frequent services were operated between Nassau and England, Canada, the United States, British colonies in the Caribbean and South America. British Overseas Airways provide services between England and Nassau via Bermuda, and between Nassau and South America via Jamaica. Both British Overseas Airways and Pan-American Airways provide daily services between Nassau and Miami with an increase in frequency of services during the winter season. Trans-Canada Airlines operate frequent services between Nassau and Toronto, Montreal and Tampa. The Nassau-Montreal service connects with the trans-Atlantic routes of this company to Scotland, London and the continent of Europe. These services are also increased during the season. The major local company, Bahamas Airways, provides frequent services to West Palm Beach via West End, Grand Bahama, and also conducts twice-weekly services to the main Out Islands. Aircraft of this company and another local company, Skyways Limited, are available for charter flights to any of the Out Islands. Besides Oakes’ Airport, there is another airport at West End, Grand Bahama, which operates from dawn to dusk only, and a private landing strip at Rock Sound, Eleuthera. At the end of 1950, there were thirteen aircraft on the Bahamas Register of Aircraft. During the year, Oakes’ Airport experienced an increase in traffic of approximately 28 per cent. over the 1949 movements. Traffic in the Nassau Control Area was also greatly increased due to the opening of Grand Bahama Airport. Internal Services. The daily airmail service to West End, Grand Bahama, has been replaced by a service operating two to three times per week. International Services. The despatch of airmails direct to Great Britain and Bermuda now takes place three to four times per week (as compared with five times per week previously). (See Note on page 27.) In 1950, the Colony’s Revenue amounted to £1,579,748 and the Expenditure to £1,658,741. The authorised Public Debt at December, 1950, was £456,000, made up of locally raised loans. The total held for repayment was £182,664. The total value of currency notes issued by the Bahamas Government through the local branch of the Royal Bank of Canada in circulation on the 31st December, 1950, was £663,800. Deposits in the Post Office Savings Bank on the 30th June, 1950, totalled £381,302. Imports (including specie) during 1950 totalled £6,150,200, the main items being foodstuffs—£1,909,545; wines and spirits—£243,885; and tobaccos—£87,326. Exports (including specie) during 1950 totalled £904,671. The principal items exported were tomatoes—£139,158; lumber—£241,840; craw- fish—£58,683; salt—£35,224; sisal—£16,886; straw and shell work—£17,819; and shell—£12,674. The total tonnage of shipping entered and cleared in the Colony in 1950 was 1,548,786, of which British tonnage totalled 970,812. The Colonial Secretary. The Attorney General. The Receiver General. In The Colonial Office List 1951 , page 74, it is stated that "In the north-west—the Scotland district—the watershed is nearer to the coast and the descent to the sea is consequently steeper." For "north-west" should be read "north-east". The Treasurer is no longer nominated by the House of Assembly. In 1951 adult suffrage was introduced and the number of electors is expected to exceed 95,000. In addition to the scholarships and exhibitions tenable at Oxford or Cambridge University, Codrington College and the Imperial College of Tropical Agriculture in Trinidad, two exhibitions are awarded for the University College of the West Indies. The membership of the Barbados Workers' Union has increased to 12,000. At the end of 1950 there were 100 Barbadian workers in the United States of America under contract and a total of 112 distributed among the islands of Bermuda, Curacao and Aruba. The principal steamship lines operating at the end of 1950 were :— Alcoa Steamship Company Incorporated, Blue Star Line, Canadian National Steamships, Compagnie Générale Trans-atlantique, Elders & Fyffes, Harrison Line, Furness Withy & Company, Limited, Moore McCormack Line, Royal Netherlands Steamship Company, and Saguenay Terminals Limited. Barbados is the headquarters of the schooner trade in the Eastern Caribbean. The landing ground at Seawell has a new hard-surfaced runway of 6,000 feet. Trans-Canada Airlines operate to and from Montreal weekly. With regard to internal telecommunications, the wire broadcasting system is provided by the Barbados Rediffusion Service Limited. During 1950 1,185,580 postal packages were despatched and 1,135,706 received. (See Note on page 27.) For 1950–51 the Revenue amounted to £2,263,927 and the Expenditure to £2,008,892. The Public Debt at the 31st March, 1951, remained at £605,360. The Sinking Fund at that date was £276,601. In 1949–50 grants and loans to the Colony under the United Kingdom Colonial Development and Welfare Acts amounted to £337,500. The figures for 1950–51 were £3,203. Customs revenue for the financial year 1950–51 amounted to $4,664,404, an increase of $722,781 over the revenue for the previous year. Duty collected on rum during the financial year was $934,742. The amount of income tax collected during the financial year was $3,675,740, an increase of $818,739 on the amount collected in the previous year. The rates of income tax vary from year to year. The main items of expenditure during the financial year 1950–51 were : Education, $1,651,566, an increase of $111,044 ; Medical Services, $1,036,729, an increase of $86,810 ; Roads and Transport, $626,684, a decrease of $107,009 ; Subsidies, Controls, etc., $1,159,452, an increase of $15,755. The currency system comprises notes issued by the Eastern Caribbean Currency Board, set up in 1951, notes previously issued by the Government and by the Governments of British Guiana and Trinidad, and United Kingdom coin of which silver is legal tender up to $9.60 (£2). There is no local issue of coin. Government currency notes continue as legal tender but are being withdrawn with the introduction of the Eastern Caribbean note issue. With this issue the currency note system of the Eastern Group of British Caribbean Territories (Barbados, British Guiana, the Leeward Islands, Trinidad and the Windward Islands) was unified in 1951. Legislative provision was also made for eventual unification of coin. The number of depositors of the Government Savings Bank at the 30th June, 1950, was 40,876, and the total amount standing to their credit was $10,471,968. (See Note on page 27.) The trade figures for goods imported during 1950, which amounted to £8,065,944, showed an increase as compared with £7,064,988 for 1949. This increase was due chiefly to the importations of greater quantities of goods and the increased values. The chief imports were animal feeding-stuffs, fish (all kinds), flour, meat (all kinds), rice, lumber, bags and sacks (empty), cotton piece goods, other cotton manufactures, artificial silk piece goods, electrical apparatus and machinery. The total value of exports was £5,736,623, as compared with £4,627,490 in 1949, the increase being largely due to the abundant sugar crop. The main exports and chief industries of the Island centre around the sugar, molasses and rum industries. The main exports compared with 1949 are set out in the following table. In addition to the fact that the Turkish Family Law is expressly saved, the old Sher'i Courts which administered the Sher'iat (or Kuranic Law) were, on the recommendation of the Turkish Affairs Committee and as a result of the Turkish Family Courts Law, 1951, replaced by the Turkish Family Courts. Instead of being presided over by the Sher'ie Hakimi (or Kadi) the new Courts have, as Presidents, Turkish Family Court Judges, the old office of Kadi being abrogated. These Courts have jurisdiction over persons of the Moslem faith in matters concerning betrothal, marriage, divorce (and matters pertaining thereto), maintenance, registration of Vakfiehs (Moslem Charitable, or otherwise inalienable grants, or trusts) and administration of Infants' Estates. Appeals lie in the usual way to the Supreme Court. The Sher'i Laws previously in force were repealed by the Turkish Family (Marriage and Divorce) Law, 1951. In addition to the salaries stated, expatriate officers receive an expatriation allowance at the rate of 15 per cent. of their salary. See pages 122–126 of The Colonial Office List 1951. The islands are in the same latitude South as London is North but there is little similarity in climate apart from hours of sunshine, which are almost identical. Mean monthly temperatures are uniformly lower but London has both higher and lower extremes. Warm spells, such as occur in a good English summer, are never experienced. Cold outbreaks may occur at almost any time of the year and the weather is generally changeable. The islands are exposed and persistent strong winds spoil many otherwise pleasant days in the summer. (The wind reaches gale force one day in five.) Annual rainfall is rather higher than in the London area. The climate is bracing. Some people find the strong winds trying though somewhat similar conditions are found in the exposed coastal districts of Scotland. Spring, autumn and winter clothing, as used in the United Kingdom, is suitable. There is no need for extra-heavy under-clothes and wind-proof outer clothing is much warmer. Mean annual temperature .. 43° F. Mean annual maximum .. 70° F. Mean annual minimum .. 19° F. Mean annual wind speed .. 15 knots Mean annual rainfall .. 30 inches Stanley had, on the 31st December, 1950, six vessels on its register aggregating 1,003 registered tons. Birth rate, 1950—15.68 per 1,000. Death rate, 1950 : 11.65 per 1,000. The Government is administered by a Governor, aided by an Executive Council composed of three ex officio members and two nominated members, and a Legislative Council composed of three ex officio members, four elected members, two nominated unofficial, and two nominated official members. The nominated members of the Legislative Council are appointed by warrant under the Royal Sign Manual and Signet. Unless the Council is dissolved earlier by the Governor, elections are held every four years. In Stanley a Government School is maintained from public funds. It provides infant, primary and secondary courses. The school-leaving age is 14, but provision is made for voluntary attendance at a continuation class for a two years' course of further education. This, in many subjects, reaches school certificate standard. The Government contributes £2 per month towards the cost of board and lodging of children from the country districts attending the Stanley or Settlement Schools. One Supervisor of Camp Education and ten Camp teachers are provided by the Government. Some of the teachers travel from house to house but seven settlements have resident teachers. The number of children receiving education in 1950 was 353. Two scholarships, for a period of three years, are awarded annually to successful candidates between the ages of 12 and 14 years at schools in England. The first scholarships commenced in February, 1943. (Note : "Camp" is the term applied locally to the country districts outside Stanley.) The Medical Service of the Colony is at present carried out by three Government Medical Officers, two in Stanley and one at Fox Bay on the West Falkland. In addition to this the Falkland Islands Company employs a doctor at Darwin on the East Falkland. The Hospital in Stanley is in charge of the Senior Medical Officer. It has, at present, 17 beds, but is in process of extension. When the work is completed in a few months' time it will accommodate 35 patients. Ante-natal and Child Welfare Clinics are well attended. The Hospital maintains a Maternity Department in which nearly all the confinements from both islands take place. Examinations of the school children take place twice a year and are combined with regular tuberculin tests. A Dental Officer is stationed at Stanley and a Dentist for the outlying farms on East and West Falklands will, it is hoped, commence duty early in 1952. The population, entirely European, is, generally speaking, of good health, but has, as frequently observed in other isolated communities, little resistance to infectious diseases imported from the South American coast, which is only five days journey by sea. Fortunately, no severe epidemic diseases of the notifiable group have occurred during the last five years. The efficiency of the Medical Service, previously entirely dependent on communication by horse or boat, has greatly improved since the introduction of an Air Service, which has proved invaluable in cases of emergency. Although there is no special Welfare Department its purposes are served by the Medical Department, Stanley Town Council, and the Labour Federation. There is an old established Benefit Club in Stanley which provides a form of insurance against unemployment through sickness of its members and assists with funeral expenses. A scheme of Children's Allowances has been introduced by the Government. PUBLIC FINANCE (See Note on page 27.) The estimated population at 31st December, 1950, was :— The Central Medical School in Suva now has a teaching staff of 3 whole time officers and 14 honorary lecturers. The whole time officers consist of the principal, assistant principal and a dental officer. The honorary lecturers include the medical and surgical specialists and other medical officers of the department. The school was founded in 1884 for the training of Fiji youths. Since 1928, however, its benefits have been available to other Western Pacific territories and its graduates, now numbering over 300, include Fijians, Fiji Indians, Western Samoans, Eastern Samoans, Tongans, Gilbertese, Ellice Islanders, Solomon Islanders, New Hebrideans, Rotumans, Nauruans, Cook Islanders and Niue Islanders. In January, 1951, 29 dental students and 37 medical students from the U.S. Trust Territory, Pacific Islands, who had been enrolled at the U.S. Naval Medical School at Guam were transferred to the Central Medical School in Suva. A larger central medical school and hostel to accommodate 150 students is being built. The number of local vessels trading within the Colony and holding sea-going certificates from the Marine Board during 1950 was :— The number of merchant vessels entered from overseas at the three ports of entry in the Colony during the year 1950 was 146, the total registered tonnage being 474,264. Overseas surface mails to New Zealand and United Kingdom via New Zealand are despatched at an average of three times each month and similarly to Australia and India, via Australia Mails to North and South America and United Kingdom via Canada are despatched approximately twice monthly. Airmails to United Kingdom, Near and Far East and Australia are despatched five times weekly and to New Zealand, North and South America and Hawaii, thrice weekly. Samoa is served weekly and the Cook Islands fortnightly. Internal radio stations have been established at the following places :—Suva, Lautoka, Nayavu, Levuka, Moturiki, Makogai, Vunisea, Lambasai Savusavu, Nambouwalu, Mbutha Bay, Rambi Island, Waiyevo (Taveuni), Ndreketi, Natewa, Wainunu, Lomaloma, Rotuma, Lakeba, Matuku Island, Kambara Island, Mbenga Island, Mothe Island, Fulanga Island, Oneata Island, Koro Island and Ngau Island (fixed stations owned and operated by the Fiji Government) : M. V. "Andi Maopa", M.V. "Viti II", M.V. "Dengei" (Fiji Government ships) : Nandi Airport, Undu Point, Ono-I-Lau, Yasawa-I-Rara (N.Z. Meteorological Stations) : Wakaya Island, Katafanga Island, Mambavatu and Naitamba (privately-owned stations). Services are confined to circuits between Suva and other Government and private stations for public correspondence and Nandi Airport and meteorological stations for weather traffic, except in emergency. The following Fiji Government radio-telephone stations were to be in operation by the end of 1951 : Moala Island, Totoya Island, Vatoa Island, Nayau Island, Vanua Vatu Island, Namuka-I-Lau Island, Waimotu (Vanua Levu) and Vunindawa (Viti Levu). The airfield is at Yundum, distant 17 miles from Bathurst. It is controlled by the Government and operated on behalf of the Ministry of Civil Aviation by International Aeradio Ltd. The Public Works Department maintains runways, buildings, power and water supplies. The Meteorological Service is operated by the West African Meteorological Service. The regular scheduled service calling at Yundum is that of the West African Airways Corporation which calls twice weekly on the route Lagos–Accra–Roberts Field–Freetown–Dakar. This service connects northwards at Dakar with the trunk routes between the United Kingdom, Europe and South America and southwards via Sierra Leone, Gold Coast and Nigeria, with South Africa. Yundum is also used as a major diversion airport for Dakar by the British Overseas Airways Corporation and Air France. (See Note on page 27.) With certain exceptions, notably in the case of females and persons seconded to the service of the island from other public services ( vide Jamaica Civil Service Widows and Orphans Pension (Amendment) Law, 1943), all salaries of £225 and over are by law subject to a deduction of 4 per cent. payable to the Widows and Orphans Pension Scheme. A housing allowance is at present payable to certain officers. A regular three-weekly steamship service is maintained from Georgetown with Kingston, Jamaica and Belize, British Honduras. There is also communication by sailing and motor vessels with the ports of Central America and the United States. A weekly air service is operated between Jamaica, Grand Cayman and Tampa, Florida. Alighting areas for flying-boat operation are maintained at Grand Cayman and Cayman Brac; the latter however, can only be used when the prevailing wind is from the south. The deferred rate for wireless messages to the United States of America is now 5d. per word. (See Note on page 27.) Plans for the reorganisation of the solar evaporated salt industry have been mooted at various times for the last century, but from 1946 onwards great efforts have been made to find a solution to the difficulties of a small industry which is compelled to sell its products on a highly competitive world market, which had over fifty owners all with rival theories of what should be done and which was crippled by lack of capital. On April 9th, 1951, the Turks Islands Salt Company Limited took over the industry, the owners of salinas and ancillary equipment and property voluntarily accepting £100,000 (including a schedule of Government equipment and property valued at £6,000) as compensation. Approximately one-third of the compensation was in cash and two-thirds in redeemable preference shares in the new Company. The new capital was made available from Colonial Development and Welfare Funds and a further sum of £60,000 is being loaned to the Company by the Colonial Development Corporation. The Government of the Dependency holds all the Ordinary shares in the Company and has a majority holding of the entire share capital and a majority on the Board of Directors. This variation of the principle of public ownership provides for a partnership of Public funds and Private capital and gives the Dependency the opportunity to acquire its major industry outright over a period of thirty years. The strong Welfare aspect of this scheme is balanced by the necessity for sound commercial management because of the highly competitive nature of the salt business. The tasks of re-organisation and of speeding up lightering and loading arrangements are now being tackled. The small landing strip at Turks Island was used by light commercial and private aircraft operating occasional services between the Island and the Dominican Republic. The landing strip at South Caicos was available to all aircraft on request or for purposes of emergency, and was used on several occasions. (See Note on page 27.) The total African Christian community is estimated to be of the order of 800,000 (not 300,000 as stated in The Colonial Office List 1951 ). In the list of districts of the Coast Province given in The Colonial Office List 1951 "Giriama" should now be read instead of "Kilifi". "Mandera" should be added to the list of districts of the Northern Province. The full title of the Chief Secretary is now "Chief Secretary and Member for Development". Other revised titles are :—The Deputy Chief Secretary and Member for Education and Labour, The Member for Agriculture and Natural Resources, and The Member for Health, Lands and Local Government. The Member for Commerce and Industry now sits on the Executive and Legislative Councils as an ex officio member. Sir Reginald Edwin Robins is no longer a member of the Authority for Development and Reconstruction. The members now are Sir Godfrey Rhodes, C.B., C.B.E., D.S.O., M.L.C., Major A. G. Keyser, D.S.O., M.L.C. and Sir Alfred Vincent, M.C.A., in addition, of course, to the Chief Secretary and Member for Development as Chairman. The Planning Committee set up in 1948 to revise the Development Programme, was reconstituted as a Standing Planning Committee in March, 1950, with the Chief Secretary as Chairman, two other official and four unofficial Members. This Committee submitted an interim report in November, 1950, and it presented in June, 1951, a comprehensive report recommending the lines of development for the remainder of the ten-year period. The African Information Services, set up at the beginning of 1951, are charged with the task of providing a centralized service for the production and employment of information material in all media designed to assist the Press Officer, District Teams and the departments of Government, so as to advance the social and economic welfare of the African peoples. The following are the main services provided by the office :— The Press Office was established in the Secretariat on June 1st, 1951. All official hand-outs and communiques are issued to the European, Asian and African Press through this office, and particular attention is now being paid towards improving the standard of the African vernacular press. Articles on the work of Government Departments and matters of general interest are prepared for local press use and for overseas, and distribution is made through the office to the local press of material prepared by the Colonial Office and the Central Office of Information. A summary of opinion on African affairs as expressed in the local press is prepared fortnightly and distributed for information only on a limited distribution list to those interested in African Affairs. This office also prepares and issues the K.I.O. Fortnightly, a publication of particular interest to farmers. Every assistance is rendered to overseas journalists visiting Kenya as well as to the press throughout the world when information on the Colony is required. “Pamoja”, a Swahili broadsheet for Africans containing an editorial on local or world affairs, is published weekly and 18,500 copies distributed. Funds and material are supplied for 14 district news sheets in local vernaculars and in some cases these are edited in the office. Books, pamphlets, leaflets and posters are prepared as required to support specific publicity campaigns. Broadcasts to Africans are given daily from a studio in the office and transmitted throughout Kenya by Messrs. Cable and Wireless, Ltd. Special broadcasts such as running commentaries on the more important football matches and athletic meetings are given periodically as well as regular quiz competitions, radio plays and other features. Three local broadcasts in Swahili are given each week from a studio in Mombasa. Funds for this service and material for the broadcasts are provided by the office. The office owns and maintains some hundred wireless sets which are distributed to community centres, information rooms, district headquarters, missions, schools, etc., for communal listening to its programmes by Africans. A Photographic Section is maintained to provide pictures needed for the illustration of the Services' publications and to prepare filmstrips for Africans. All photographs in a library of some 12,000 prints are available to the general public, visiting journalists, etc. Prints are supplied to Government Departments for displays at Agricultural Shows and other exhibitions and to community centres for wall display. The office owns and maintains some 90 filmstrip projectors which are placed in community centres throughout the Colony. In addition to locally produced filmstrips these are supplied with strips obtained from the United Kingdom. A Production Unit makes 16mm. educational and comic films and newsreels for Africans. A central film library is maintained consisting of locally produced films together with those supplied by the Central Office of Information and the Colonial Film Unit and some purchased from commercial sources. In addition to the mobile information units this library supplies some 105 projection posts showing to Africans throughout the Colony. 35mm. copies of "British News" received from the Central Office of Information are supplied to commercial cinemas. Seven Mobile Information Units are attached to Provincial Commissioners' offices in the four main provinces. They are constantly on safari, working under the direction of the Provincial Administration, and give cinema shows in the evenings, and lectures supported by filmstrip shows during the daytime. The majority of the shows are for Africans but European and Asian schools also receive visits from time to time. In addition to its own publications the office distributes some 10,000 copies of each issue of the Colonial Office publication in Swahili and English, "Siku Hizi", free to individual Africans, community centres, schools, information rooms, district headquarters, missions and employers of large labour forces. Posters, booklets, etc., received from the Colonial Office and the Central Office of Information are also distributed to various lists of addressees in the Colony, selected according to the type of material. The average weekly output amounts to some 35,000 items. The office undertakes translation work for other Government departments in five local vernaculars. Local Native Councils are now known as African District Councils. The Social Welfare Organisation has been renamed The Community Development Organisation. It is still, however, a branch of the African Affairs Group. It has its Headquarters at Jeanes School, Kabete, where the Central Composite Training Centre is also situated. The Commissioner for Social Welfare has been re-designated the Commissioner for Community Development. He is directly responsible to the Chief Native Commissioner for the policy with regard to Community Development including African Information Services and the Jeanes School. In general the word "Welfare", previously occurring in the designation of officials dealing with community development, has been replaced by the words "Community Development"—e.g. African Welfare Workers are now known as Community Development Assistants. Remedial welfare work is now organised separately. The Colony's road system consists of approximately 17,000 miles of road maintained as follows :— 4,680 by Public Works Department. 2,628 by European District Councils. 272 by Municipal Councils. 481 by Royal National Parks Trustees. 477 by Forestry Department. 8,500 by African District Councils and the Administration. 237 miles of the P.W.D. roads system are bitumen surfaced. The great North road passes through Kenya, entering the Colony from Tanganyika and the South at Namanga and passing through Nairobi, Nakuru and Kisumu to cross the border into Uganda at Busia. The Nairobi-Nakuru section of this road (approximately 100 miles in length) is bitumenised and work is at present in progress on bitumenising the seventeen miles between Nairobi and Athi River. Sixteen miles of bitumen road to the west of Nakuru are practically finished and work has started on the construction of the road westward towards Londiani. Work has also started on the construction of a road on a new alignment leading northward from Thika to Sagana. The recently realigned Nairobi-Kiambu road is scheduled for bitumen surfacing this year (1951). Work on the realignment of the road from Mombasa to Mackinnon Road (55 miles) continues and construction of the bridges carrying the new road over the Maji-ya-Chumvi river and railway line is in progress. Apart from eight miles of bitumen surface near Mackinnon Road, the new road will not be bitumenised for the present. The shipping services listed under this heading in The Colonial Office List 1951 have been subject to some modification and certain new services have been instituted. (See Note on page 27.) In 1950, the Revenue amounted to £13,244,019 and the Expenditure to £12,503,798. The Public Debt totalled £13,710,600. In 1949, Revenue amounted to £13,030,651 and Expenditure to £10,761,676. The Public Debt totalled £14,010,600. The Standard Bank of South Africa, Ltd. now maintains an Agency at Molo as well as at Thika. Barclays Bank (D., C. & O.) maintains an Agency at Thika as well as at Kilindini. The export of agricultural and animal produce reached a total value of £9,635,894 in 1949. Fishing is carried on off the sea-coast of Kenya, and in Lake Victoria, Lake Rudolf and other smaller lakes and rivers. Adequate supplies for consumption in the Colony are available. The main difficulties are distribution and retailing facilities. Fishery organisation is going ahead. In 1949 the Customs Departments of Kenya, Uganda and Tanganyika were amalgamated under the title of the East African Customs and Excise Departments as an East Africa High Commission Service with Headquarters in Mombasa and Regional Offices in Mombasa, Kampala and Dar-es-Salaam. Identical customs tariffs were enacted in Kenya, Uganda and Tanganyika in 1923 and revised in 1930. Surcharges were introduced during the recent war and in 1946 these were incorporated in the Kenya Tariff. In 1948, however, the equivalent of the war-time surcharges were removed from the import duties on certain food stuffs, apparel, cotton piece goods, bicycles and parts and motor cars and parts. Subsequent amendments to the tariff have been made with the intention of adjusting anomalies assisting reconstruction by removal of duties on certain builder's materials, and attempting to offset the high and rising cost of essential consumer goods such as shirts, underwear, paraffin, khaki drill and blankets by the removal or reduction of duties on these goods. The last mentioned alterations were made in 1950 by Kenya and Uganda, but similar alterations were not made in Tanganyika, and the tariffs of the three East African territories, while possessing a main common structure, now differ in minor respects. The basic fiscal rate is 22 per cent. ad valorem assessed on the value which is taken as being the price which the goods would fetch on sale in the open market in the Colony if delivered at the port of importation, freight, insurance and all costs charges and expenses having been paid by the seller. Specific duties are charged on goods which lend themselves to such a form of assessment, the more important of such goods being potable spirits, cigarettes and tobacco, motor spirit tyres and tubes, matches and cement. In the case of certain articles such as piece goods wearing apparel and cycles, minimum specific duties have been fixed as an alternative to an ad valorem rate in order to safeguard the revenue against abnormally cheap supplies. The free list includes the chief development imports such as machinery; road, rail, air and water transport equipment; coal, coke and fuel oil; packing materials; explosives; insecticides and agricultural and mining equipment; and builders' requisites and sanitaryware. Exemption from duty is also allowed in the case of drugs, medicines and disinfectants; shirts, vests, socks, stockings, pullovers, cardigans, jumpers; paraffin; filters and refrigerators, surgical and scientific instruments; educational requisites; religious articles; music and literature and bona fide baggage brought by passengers for personal and household use. Provision is made in the tariff for the imposition of what are known as "suspended" duties on certain commodities which are produced and manufactured in one or more of the territories—Kenya, Uganda and Tanganyika. These duties may be imposed, varied or revoked unilaterally thus allowing a modified control by each territory over the degree of protection afforded to a number of the local products. By a tripartite Customs Agreement in 1927, a system of inter-territorial transfers of imported goods was introduced between Kenya, Uganda and Tanganyika. The effect of this agreement is virtually to remove customs barriers between the three territories the duty being credited to the receiving territory by a transfer form procedure. The Congo Basin Treaty obligations prevent the grant of Imperial Preference. In 1950 the value for Kenya trade imports excluding imports on Government account, railway material and goods in transit and transhipment was £27,979,691. In the same period the value for exports (domestic exports and re-exports) was £20,737,006. N.B.—Figures for Kenya are for the first time shown separately and do not include Uganda. In addition the figures for net imports do not conform to those of previous years since they are calculated on a different basis. They now represent goods imported for consumption or warehousing in Kenya, including goods which are subsequently re-exported outside East Africa. In addition the value of goods transferred during the year from one East African territory to another has been deducted from the figures for the year's imports of the territory from which they have come and added to those of the territory to which they have been sent, even though the goods in question may have been imported from abroad during a previous year. The figures of re-exports to foreign countries included in the Trade Exports figure for the year merely represent the figures of imported goods which were subsequently re-exported to places outside East Africa and do not include re-exports to another East African territory. Accordingly the Trade Exports figure for 1950 does not conform to those shown for preceding years. In 1950 the main Net Imports into Kenya were:—Food products, beverages and Tobacco £2,327,168; Chemicals and allied products £1,505,982; Rubber and manufactures thereof £724,313; Textiles £3,310,395; Clothing of all materials and miscellaneous textile goods £2,098,525; Products for heating, lighting, power and lubricants £4,282,291; Non-metallic minerals and manufactures thereof £1,730,891; Base metals and manufactures thereof £4,635,195; Machinery, apparatus, appliances and vehicles £8,464,268. The Principal Countries of Origin were:—United Kingdom £19,605,158; Bahrein Islands £1,905,267; India £2,744,246; Union of South Africa £1,428,638; Other parts of the British Commonwealth £655,070 (Total British Commonwealth £26,338,379); Italy £568,188; Japan £376,646; Persia £1,633,839; United States of America £1,606,761; Other Foreign Countries £3,554,051 (Total Foreign Countries £7,739,485). The Principal Domestic Exports of Kenya during 1950 were:—Coffee £3,559,553; Tea £1,333,854; Other food products, beverages and tobacco £1,905,439; Fatty substances and waxes, animal and vegetable £149,828; Sodium carbonate £860,511; Wattle bark extract £978,945; Hides, Skins, leather and manufactures thereof £1,978,973; Cotton lint £227,823; Pyrethrum £330,723; Sisal £4,061,168; Gold—unworked £355,979. The Chief Countries of Destination were:—United Kingdom £6,056,845 (Total British Commonwealth £10,437,649); United States of America £2,267,109; (Total Foreign Countries £6,621,528). During the year 1950 the cost of education in the Colony was $565,359. A cost-of-living allowance is now paid on salaries as follows :— 50 per cent. on the first £100. 30 per cent. on the second £100. 20 per cent. on the third £100. Communications between the Colony and the United Kingdom are maintained by vessels of the Harrison Line which call approximately once a month at Antigua and St. Kitts to discharge cargo and load sugar and cotton for the United Kingdom. During the crop season for sugar more frequent calls are made. These vessels call about four times during the last five months of each year at Montserrat in order to load cotton for the United Kingdom. In 1950, a vessel belonging to the Compagnie Générale Transatlantique also maintained a regular service between Antigua and the United Kingdom. Irregular inter-island services are provided by schooners, sloops and two converted M.L.Cs. British West Indian Airways maintain a thrice-weekly service between Antigua and St. Kitts, and regular services from Antigua to other parts of the British West Indies. The service to Jamaica connects with the British Overseas Airways Corporation's service to the United Kingdom. Pan-American World Airways maintain a service between Puerto Rico and Trinidad, which calls thrice weekly northbound and southbound at Antigua and at St. Thomas in the U.S. Virgin Islands whence Tortola in the British Virgin Islands may easily be reached by motor launch. K.L.M. maintain a service between Curacao and St. Martin, which calls at Guadeloupe and St. Kitts en route. In 1950 the total imports of the Leeward Islands were valued at £2,756,378 and the total exports at £2,495,907. Revenue amounted to £1,226,567 and Expenditure to £1,307,908. Following unification of the currency note system in the Eastern Group of British Caribbean Territories (Barbados, British Guiana, the Leeward Islands, Trinidad and the Windward Islands) in 1951, the currency includes notes issued by the Eastern Caribbean Currency Board. Legislative provision exists for the eventual issue of Eastern Caribbean coinage but, meanwhile, British silver continues in currency.
