SITUATION AND AREA The table showing the areas of the various divisions of the Gold Coast under this heading in The Colonial Office List 1951 , should be replaced by the following :— The Southern Togoland Territorial Council fulfils functions similar to those of the Joint Provincial Council of Chiefs in the Colony, in respect of the southern section of Togoland under United Kingdom Trusteeship which is administered with the Colony. In 1948 a Commission of Enquiry was appointed to investigate the underlying causes of certain disturbances which had occurred earlier in that year. The Commission's recommendations included various proposals for constitutional reform ; and as a result of His Majesty's Government's comments on the Commission's report, a fully representative all-African Committee was appointed to examine them. The Committee reported to the Governor in August, 1949 ; and after detailed study of its proposals a new constitution was drawn up which came into effect in January, 1951. The new constitution makes provision for a Legislative Assembly legislating for the whole of the Gold Coast and elected partly by constituencies and partly from the Territorial Councils of Chiefs. It also provides for an Executive Council of Ministers chosen from and approved by the Assembly. Three of the Ministers are officials. Certain reserve powers are retained by the Governor. The first elections, under a system of universal suffrage which was worked out for the constituencies, took place in February, 1951. The social and economic history of the Gold Coast in the twentieth century has been accelerated by a number of influences and in particular by the spread of education and the development of the cocoa industry. The prosperity of the various African companies for nearly four centuries depended in large measure on the slave trade. Its suppression in the nineteenth century, coinciding with the Ashanti wars, reduced commerce on the coast to straits from which it revived only temporarily when the security of Captain Maclean's administration fostered the development of the palm oil trade. Vegetable oils remained for several decades one of the principal objects of commerce on the coast. This and other trades were, however, displaced in importance by the cocoa industry which from small beginnings rapidly expanded in the new century until the Gold Coast became the world's largest producer, and cocoa the key to the country's prosperity. This prosperity is being threatened by swollen shoot, a serious and contagious virus disease of cocoa whose depredations constitute a grave menace to the economy of the country. A strenuous campaign based on the best scientific advice and research is being waged by Government and slow but steady progress is being made in the eradication of the diseased trees and the rehabilitation of the devastated areas. The marketing of cocoa has been undertaken since 1947 by the Government sponsored Gold Coast Cocoa Marketing Board. From the days of the Portuguese, gold attracted to the coast adventurers of many nations; in recent times the prosperity of the Gold Coast has been increased by the discovery and working of diamonds, manganese and bauxite, and by the rapid expansion of the timber industry. Takoradi Harbour, built during the Governorship of Sir Gordon Guggisberg and opened in 1928, has become the economic gateway of the country and contributes greatly to its prosperity and development. The pioneers of education in the Gold Coast were the Wesleyan and Basel missions of the early nineteenth century. They were followed by many others, the Anglican, Presbyterian and Roman Catholic being prominent among them. Education was for many years left entirely to the missions, assisted in the latter part of the century by increasing subventions from Government. Throughout the present century Government, while continuing its aid to the missions, has also built many schools of its own, and Native Authorities have taken an increasingly prominent part in the extension of schooling facilities. Government has, in these and other ways, pursued a policy of wide educational expansion of which the opening of Achimota College in 1924 afforded remarkable evidence, and the establishment of the University College in 1948 a proof of a continued progress. The Constitutional instruments providing for the Government of the Gold Coast and setting up the constitution which came into effect on the 1st January, 1951, are the Gold Coast (Constitution) Order in Council, 1951; the Letters Patent, passed under the Great Seal of the Realm (constituting the office of Governor and Commander-in-Chief); the Northern Territories of the Gold Coast Order in Council, 1950; and the Instructions passed under the Royal Sign Manual and Signet to the Governor and Commander-in-Chief. The Executive Council consists of the Governor as President; three ex officio members, namely the Chief Secretary, the Attorney-General and the Financial Secretary; and not less than eight representative members, who are members of the Legislative Assembly proposed by the Governor and approved by the Assembly. The Leader of the Government Business in the Legislative Assembly is elected by a majority of the Executive Council from among their number. The Governor at his discretion may charge any member of the Executive Council with the responsibility of Government Departments. Members are known as Ministers. There is provision for Ministerial Secretaries to be appointed from among members of the Legislative Assembly and for a Permanent Secretary in each Ministry who will be a public officer appointed by the Governor. The Legislative Assembly legislates for all Territories of the Gold Coast and consists of a Speaker, three ex officio members, namely the Chief Secretary, the Attorney-General and the Financial Secretary, three special members who represent the commercial interests of whom only one is permitted to vote, and three special members who represent mining interests also with only one vote. The seventy-five elected members are made up of thirty-three rural members, five municipal members, eleven territorial members elected by the Joint Provincial Council, six territorial members elected by the Asanteman Council, nineteen territorial members elected by the Electoral College of the Northern Territories formed out of the Northern Territories Territorial Council and one territorial member elected by the Southern Togoland Council. The rural members are those elected by the people of constituencies whose boundaries were laid down in the Elections Ordinance, 1950, made under a special Order in Council for the purpose. This Ordinance laid down electoral procedure, of which the main provision is that elections in rural constituencies should take place by secret ballot in two stages. In the first stage primary elections are held to choose representatives for local electoral colleges, which in the second stage choose one, or in certain cases two, representatives for each constituency. In order to qualify for registration to vote or for nomination as a candidate, an individual man or woman must be aged 21, a British subject and have a residential qualification of a minimum of six months. Literacy is not imposed as a qualification except in the case of candidates for the secondary elections. The four municipalities of Accra, Kumasi, Sekondi/Takoradi and Cape Coast are represented by the urban members who are elected by direct election. Primary education consists of a six-year course from which pupils may proceed further to a four-year senior primary course. Secondary Education. In December, 1950, 426 candidates from Government-assisted institutions, including the Achimota School, passed the overseas School Certificate Examination of the University of Cambridge Local Examination Syndicate. The University College of the Gold Coast. There were 182 students taking Intermediate and Degree Courses and 28 taking the Associate Course at the Institute of Education during the 1950-51 session. The College of Technology, Science and Arts, Kumasi. The Ordinance to establish this College was passed in September, 1951; and some students were expected to be in residence in January, 1952. The college will play an important part in the structure of education in the Gold Coast and will eventually have an enrolment of 2,000 students in residence. It will provide courses in engineering, agriculture, commercial subjects, medical auxiliary services, teacher-training in specialist subjects, and in other professions at post-secondary levels. The Public Relations Department is now organised in seven sections : Administrative, Publications, Programmes, Broadcast Engineering, Cinema, Films and Photographic. The Broadcast Engineering Section is responsible for the efficient running and maintenance of 21 Wire Rediffusion Stations and the relaying of B.B.C. and Gold Coast programmes to over 10,000 subscribers. The Gold Coast Broadcasting Station broadcasts daily except Sundays and Public Holidays between 10.30 a.m. and 12 noon and between 3.15 p.m. and 6 p.m., on 49.6 and 61.04 metres respectively. The purpose of the Films Section (the Gold Coast Film Unit) is to focus interest, through films, on matters of national concern and to project the Gold Coast to the outside world. "Amenu's Child", one of the films made in 1950, won an award at the Venice International Film Festival. During 1951 the Unit produced three documentary films, the subjects being clean water supply, Post Office savings facilities and juvenile delinquency. Shortage of Medical Officers is still acute, but the position has been somewhat eased by an amendment to the Medical Practitioners' and Dentists' Registration Ordinance whereby temporary licences can be issued to doctors with registrable qualifications to practise in localities where their services are needed. Out-patients treated in Gold Coast hospitals in 1950 numbered 947,231 ; 734 major operations were performed at the Gold Coast Hospital in Accra. The new Midwifery Training School at Kumasi now accommodates 32 pupil midwives. The Nurses Training College at Accra is functioning well. In 1950, 14 student nurses passed the State Registration Examination, making the total number of passes since the commencement of the examination, 28. Of the three proposed rural health centres the one at Kpandu has been completed and a Senior Health Sister posted there. The sites have been acquired and contracts placed for a second centre to be constructed at Effiduasi in Ashanti and a third at Bimbilla in the Northern Territories. The Medical Field Unit which is being organised into mass treatment teams is proving very useful. Its main function is to combat major endemic diseases such as yaws and trypanosomiasis and epidemic diseases such as cerebro-spinal meningitis and small-pox wherever possible. There are three mobile dispensaries under the charge of Government-trained nurses based on Winneba, Sunyani and Tamale respectively. These dispensaries which are an experiment are proving rather costly owing to the bad roads over which they travel and the frequent breakdowns resulting therefrom. In the rural areas particular attention is being paid by the Public Health authorities to village layouts, the protection of water supplies and the disposal of refuse. An employment exchange has now been opened in Tamale, in addition to those at Accra, Kumasi, and Takoradi. The senior staff of the Labour Department has been increased. On the 1st April, 1951, there were 73 registered trade unions whose total paid-up membership was 23,726. This compares with 64 unions and 17,985 members on the 1st April, 1950. To the end of April, 1951, 1,210 wells, 10 reservoirs, and 10 bore-holes had been completed in rural areas and in addition major piped supplies to seven towns were completed or in hand. The mileage of all-weather roads maintained by the Public Works Department is now 3,685 miles, and that of roads maintained by the Political Administration, 2,928. The gross earnings of the Railway and Takoradi and Accra Harbour Administration for the year 1950-51 were £3,262,611. The Gold Coast is connected with the United Kingdom by British Overseas Airways Corporation, with the United States of America and South Africa by Pan-American World Airways and with France, Portugal and Spain by airlines of the respective countries. West African Airways Corporation, besides operating an internal service, operate a coastal service to Dakar, where connection can be made with South America by B.O.A.C. and other international airlines. A W.A.A.C. service also operates to the Anglo-Egyptian Sudan. Accra Airport, situated four-and-a-half miles north-east of Accra, is the Gold Coast international airport and is the terminus of the B.O.A.C. London-British West Africa service. It also handles P.A.W.A. aircraft operating between New York and Johannesburg, Air France aircraft operating between Dakar and Brazzaville, Transportes Aereos Portugueses aircraft operating between Lisbon and Lourenço Marques and, occasionally, Aviacion y Comercio aircraft operating between Madrid and Fernando Po or Bata. There are three other aerodromes—at Takoradi, Kumasi and Tamale—and internal services to them by W.A.A.C. from Accra connect the western Gold Coast, Ashanti and the Northern Territories to the capital. The four aerodromes have all-weather tarmac runways. There is now also a direct air parcel post service between the United Kingdom and the Gold Coast. There are aeronautical radio-telecommunication stations with radio-beacons at Accra, Takoradi, Kumasi, and Tamale, with VHF and HF cathode-ray direction-finders at Tamale. The Revenue of the Gold Coast for the year 1950–51 amounted to £20,861,032 and the Expenditure to £17,838,968. (These figures exclude Railway finances.) The funded debt of the Gold Coast amounts to £8,410,000 and at the 31st March, 1951 the total of the statutory sinking funds for the redemption of public loans stood at £2,162,474. There is also the sum of £1,106,967 in a Supplementary Sinking Fund, to which it is proposed to add £1,500,000, from the present surplus balances. Exports, including re-exports, were valued at £76,385,820. The value of exports to the United Kingdom was £30,651,297, to other British countries, £4,819,108, and to elsewhere, £40,915,415. Customs Receipts. In 1950 receipts amounted to £12,023,573. Shipping Entered and Cleared. In 1950 the total tonnage amounted to 6,456,280, of which British tonnage totalled 4,068,493. In December, 1950 there were 72 vessels registered at Malta, the gross tonnage being 19,838. In 1950 there were 111 primary schools with 43,613 pupils. The number of literacy classes fell to 82 during the year, the attendance being 1,200. The following smaller towns and villages have an efficient system of sewage disposal : Cottonera, Sliema, St. Julians, Msida, Pieta, Kalkara, Zabbar, Hamrun, Qormi, Balzan, Lija, Attard, Birkirkara, Mdina, Rabat, Tarxien, Pawla, Zejtun, Ghaxaq and Luqa. During the financial year 1950–51, interceptors from Zebbug to Siggiewi, and Ghaxaq to Gudja were continued, and delivery mains started from the proposed pumping stations at Birzebbuga and Dingli to Ghaxaq and Rabat respectively. Street sewers have been laid in the villages of Mellieha, Zebbug, Mosta, Naxxar, and new sections in Hamrun and Birkirkara. Small extensions have been carried out in Qormi, Rabat, Pawla, Tarxien, Zabbar, Gzira, Pieta and St. Julians. With regard to the general scheme for the collection and disposal of domestic refuse, two consignments of the necessary machinery were delivered during 1950 and the delivery of the remaining consignment is expected by the end of 1951. Xaghra should be added to the list of villages in Gozo supplied with electric current. There are now 4,724 telephone exchange lines with 7,231 stations. The latest population estimates, at the 6th May, 1951, are as follows :— Europeans —37,220. Asiatics —2,551. Coloured —1,085. Africans Male —838,489. Female —835,495. European. At the end of 1950 there were 22 Government schools in the territory. Of these, seven offered primary education up to Standard 5 and secondary education up to Form III. Two others, while offering the full primary range, continued to Form II only. Eight schools provided instruction only within the primary range, four from K.G. to Standard 5 and four from K.G. to Standard 2. There were five Infants' Schools. The total enrolment at Government schools was 4,748. Certain controlled schools under private management catered for children below the permissive school age and others offered instruction within the Government school range. In addition the Lusaka Dominican Convent High School offered secondary education up to Cambridge School Certificate standard (Form IV). The total enrolment at controlled schools under private management was 1,141 including 229 children below the permissive school age. Boarding accommodation was available at four Government schools and four private schools. In addition there were, in connection with Government schools, three aided private hostels. By arrangement with the Southern Rhodesia Education Department correspondence tuition was provided as required for 61 children (mainly six to eight years old) living more than three miles from the nearest day school. Coloured and Asiatic. Ketapola School, under Government management, offered education for coloured children in the primary range. There were four private government-aided Indian schools. African. In 1950 there were 1,273 schools receiving grants-in-aid including 96 native authority schools. There are 50 Government schools. The estimated expenditure on African Education for 1951 is £498,974, of which £252,150 is grants-in-aid for recurrent expenditure. In addition, the sum of £159,807 is estimated to be spent under the Colonial Development and Welfare Scheme. With regard to wireless programmes, the small-scale rediffusion experiment which was initiated in the main African area in Lusaka has proved very successful. Owing to an improvement in the housing position it was possible to fill some vacancies in the Broadcasting (Engineers) Section of the Information Department and with the delivery of equipment the development of the transmission side of broadcasting has shown very satisfactory progress. The 15 kw. transmitter has been installed. Plans for extensions to studios and transmitters which will allow simultaneous broadcasting in several languages have been implemented. Now that mobile recording equipment has arrived programmes of African plays and music are being expanded and new and outstanding material is being provided. With regard to the development of the tourist industry, for which the Information Department is also responsible, a big-game safari scheme for a limited number of hunters is now in operation. The number of persons in employment in mines, sawmills, railways and the Public Works Department in the territory in 1950 was 6,250 Europeans and 57,300 Africans. A further 8,000 Europeans and 125,000 Africans (estimated) were engaged in other forms of employment in the territory. In the principal industry in the country (copper mining) the average monthly wages, exclusive of bonuses, cost-of-living allowance and overtime, etc., for Europeans were £48.48 (surface) and £54.02 (underground) and for Africans 62s. 2d. (surface) and 75s. 11d. (underground). To these earnings, which represent little more than half of the total remuneration, must be added the value of sub-economic rents of housing etc., enjoyed by Europeans and free housing and food provided for Africans. Wage rates in other industries and occupations continued to rise during 1950 but so did the cost of living. The index stood at 156 at the end of the year (1939 = 100). The year brought two major disputes between the African Mineworkers' Trade Union and the four Copper Mining Companies. The first, relating to wage increases, was settled but the second, relating to the question of a profit-sharing scheme for African employees, was unresolved at the end of the year. Disputes applying to the whole Territory occurred between the Northern Rhodesia African Drivers' Trade Union and the Associated Chambers of Commerce and Industry, and between the Northern Rhodesia African Shop Assistants' Trade Union and the same body during the year. Both were settled. During the year there were three strikes by European workers and 71 by African workers. In the case of the latter the strikes were almost all of short duration, and were resolved on the spot by local Labour Officers. The total man-days lost by African workers amounted to 6,124 in strikes involving 4,777 workers. 1,732 Europeans lost 2,522 man-days. The dispute inherited from 1949 between the Mine Workers' Union, supported by the African Mineworkers' Trade Union and the four Copper Mining Companies, over shorter working hours in the copper mining industry, was submitted to a Board of Inquiry which sat at Kitwe in September. The Board's report was published later in the year, and recommended that the normal weekly working hours should not be reduced to 40, but that they should not exceed 48, which they do at present, in some cases. The Board also recommended an extra annual paid holiday, and extended travelling time for European employees proceeding on leave. The Board also advised against the introduction of compulsory arbitration for disputes in the mining industry following upon the failure of conciliation. Trade Unionism continued to spread amongst African workers, and by the end of the year there were seven Trade Unions. The number of European employee representative bodies remained at four, with a like number of Associations representing employers. As a result of negotiations between the Northern Rhodesia Master Builders' and Allied Trades Association and the Northern Rhodesia General Workers' Trade Union, a very comprehensive agreement covering wages and conditions of employment was reached. This agreement lays down, for the first time in the building industry, uniform rates of wages, a 48-hour week, payment rate of time and a half for overtime, three paid Public Holidays per year, and provision for paid annual leave. The Agreement also provides for the payment to all employees of an inclusive wage in place of the old arrangement of cash wage plus food ration. The inclusive wage now includes a cash equivalent of rations and, in order to assist labourers in the new idea of feeding themselves, this cash equivalent is paid weekly. The balance of wages is paid at the end of the month or the ticket, as the case may be. This experiment appears to be working very well, and it also seems to have some effect in combating absenteeism. A further big step forward in industrial relations has been taken by both sides in the building industry in the setting up of a Joint Industrial Council. One of the most important Ordinances affecting both African and European labour introduced during the year was the Silicosis Ordinance (No. 27 of 1950), which was brought into operation on 1st November, 1950. On 10th July, 1950, the Apprenticeship (Amendment) Ordinance (No. 6 of 1950) was enacted. This was designed to prevent articles and certificates of apprenticeship being given except under the Ordinance. The scope of the Apprenticeship Ordinance was considerably widened on 3rd October, 1950, by the schedule of trades being made applicable to all industries and undertakings, including the Government of Northern Rhodesia. The Employment of Natives (Amendment) Ordinance (No. 9 of 1950) enacted on 21st July, 1950, provided that an African employee might, with the approval of the Commissioner for Labour and Mines and with his own consent, be charged rent for housing provided for him by his employer. This amendment was introduced to meet the wishes of Africans for an inclusive wage system and approval was given in the first instance only in respect of the higher grades of mine employees. The Employment of Women, Young Persons and Children (Amendment) Ordinance (No. 49 of 1950) was introduced to carry out more effectively the intention of Ordinance No. 45 of 1949, to prevent the employment of young persons between the ages of twelve and sixteen in industrial undertakings without permission. This Ordinance had not come into operation at the end of the year. Under the provisions of the Trade Unions and Trade Disputes Ordinance, 1949, the Trade Unions (Registration) Regulations, 1950, were made by the Governor in Council on 13th May, 1950. The Regulations prescribed the manner of application for registration of a Trade Union, the forms to be used, the making of annual returns and the fees payable. The Employment of Natives Regulations were amended on 4th July, 1950, to permit the Director of Medical Services and the Commissioner for Labour and Mines to approve, subject to certain safeguards, temporary buildings to be used for a period of not more than ten years, provided that each year after the second year a Labour Officer and a Medical Officer jointly certified that these buildings were fit for human habitation. The staff of the Labour Branch of the Department, excluding officers engaged purely on clerical duties, was 20, in the Trade Testing branch three officers, in the Apprenticeship branch one officer; the Workmen's Compensation Commissioner, who was also a member of the Department, performed the duties of Controller of Apprenticeship in addition to his other duties. For the purpose of the Workmen's Compensation Ordinance a workman is now, with certain exceptions, an employee whose earnings do not exceed £1,200 per annum (as compared with £950 per annum previously). Government employs three women Welfare Officers and one male Welfare Officer, although no department of Social Welfare has yet been formed. The Salvation Army operates a Home of Refuge for European children and an Old Men's Home. Both Homes are at Ndola. An additional children's home was opened during 1951 and is operated by the Diocese of Northern Rhodesia. This home is also at Ndola. African welfare in urban areas is very largely the concern of local Government authorities and of large employers of labour, who employ both European and African Welfare Officers. The duties of these officers include the organisation of sport and general recreational activities, youth clubs, handicrafts, libraries, etc. A number of patriotic and charitable bodies exist. All appeals for charitable purposes are conducted by the Central Charities Committee, a non-official body set up for the purpose. Compassionate and old age allowances are payable to Europeans who fulfil the necessary conditions. The Rhodes-Livingstone Institute was founded in 1937 by Sir Hubert Young to undertake sociological research in Central Africa. Its funds are drawn partly from private donations and partly from a Government grant and it has also received a grant from Colonial Development and Welfare funds. During 1950, members of the Institute were studying the Lunda, Yao, Shona and Ndebele peoples, besides problems of African urban life and the economic life of the Tonga people. The Institute also publishes a number of occasional papers on anthropology and related subjects. The Air Pilot of Northern Rhodesia (1950 Edition) together with the Air Law (1951 Edition) provide detailed information on aviation in Northern Rhodesia. One International and three National Airports lying along the North/South corridor route throughout the territory provide Customs, Health and Immigration controls as well as fuel, Air Traffic Control, radio and radio navigation aids. In all, 42 airfields are maintained, of which 14 are constructed for use in all weathers. Central African Airways provide both regional and internal scheduled services with a fleet of aircraft consisting of 7 Vikings, 5 Doves and 4 Beavers. They operate routes as follows :— (a) Internal (i) Lusaka, Fort Jameson—Thrice weekly each way (with additional weekly excursion to Monkey Bay). (ii) Lusaka, Livingstone—Twice weekly each way. (iii) Lusaka, Mankoya, Mongu, Kalabo, Balovale, Livingstone—Twice weekly. (iv) Lusaka, Broken Hill*, Ndola, Kasama, Abercorn—Five times weekly each way. * Flagstop. (i) Johannesburg, Salisbury, Lusaka, Ndola, Kasama, Abercorn, Tabara, Nairobi. (ii) Johannesburg, Livingstone, Lusaka, Ndola—Twice weekly each way. (iii) Johannesburg, Blantyre, Dar-es-Salaam, Nairobi—Twice weekly. (iv) Fort Jameson, Lilongwi, Blantyre—Twice weekly. East African Airways connect Fort Jameson with Nairobi, Salisbury and Blantyre twice weekly. Zambesi Airways is, at present, the only Charter operator in the Territory and is based at Livingstone, using Rapides, Fox Moths and Fairchild aircraft. The Northern Rhodesia Flying Club retains its main activities on the copperbelt but with zonal branches at Lusaka and Livingstone. The daily mail service by rail between post offices on the line of rail is now supplemented by air mails between certain main centres. The South African Airways (Springbok Service) handling airmails for overseas countries now operates three times a week. There are now 66 post offices (including one mobile post office), 15 of which are money order offices and 56 telegraph offices. The main telegraph and trunk route lies alongside the railway line from the Victoria Falls to Chingola with branches from Kitwe to Mufulira, and from Ndola to Luanshya and the Belgian Congo Border. There is also a radio telephone link between Lusaka and Fort Jameson. Wireless stations at Abercorn, Balovale, Broken Hill, Chinsali, Feira, Fort Jameson, Fort Rosebery, Isoka, Kasama, Kasempa, Kawambwa, Livingstone, Lundazi, Luwingu, Mankoya, Mkoshi, Mongu, Mpika, Mporokoso, Mumbwa, Mwinilunga, Nakonde, Namwala, Ndola, Petauke, Ridgeway (Lusaka), Senanga, Serenje, Sesheke, Solwezi and Shiwangandu maintain a public service. The actual Revenue in 1950 was £12,059,220 and the Expenditure £10,591,694 (excluding development). The estimated figures for 1951 were £14,417,630 and £14,375,310 (excluding development) respectively. Loan expenditure for development purposes was £2,213,311 during 1950 and the total of such expenditure to the end of 1950 was £5,369,571. The estimated loan expenditure for development during 1951 was £1,586,303. With regard to rates of income tax given under this sub-heading in The Colonial Office List 1951 , the deduction allowed from total income in respect of children is now £150 for each child. The maximum deduction in respect of Life, Accident and Sickness Insurance is now also £150. The Customs Revenue is expected to exceed £1,925,000 in 1951. The main heads of expenditure during 1950 were :— Development and Welfare (includes loan) .. .. .. 3,552,231 The main heads of estimated expenditure during 1951 were :— The public debt as at 31st December, 1950, was £4,637,000. The total balance in the funds as at the 31st December, 1950, was £425,539. The total cattle population in 1950 was 907,000, an increase of $4\frac{1}{2}\%$ on the previous year. 136,000 were owned by Europeans and 771,000 by Africans. 600,000 of the African-owned cattle were in the Southern and Barotse Provinces. Cattle prices were increased during the year, and 32,000 head were sold for slaughter. Supplies from Barotseland are increasing, and buying is now effected by the trade. An outbreak of Foot and Mouth Disease was controlled by the inoculation of 40,000 head with a heat-attenuated live virus. 20,000 doses of phenanthridinium were issued for the control of trypanosomiasis, and 270,000 doses of anthrax vaccine were supplied. The greater portion of the territory is undeveloped agriculturally. The indigenous population is self-supporting in its food supply, nearly the whole of its requirements being produced in the village gardens. There is little general trade in food commodities except in the railway belt where the surplus native maize crop is marketed by the Maize Control Board. This surplus in 1950 amounted to 359,000 bags. The staple native cereals are finger millet ( Eleusine coracana ), Kaffir corn ( Sorghum ) and maize. The millet is produced over extensive tracts of the territory on a system of shifting cultivation whereby grain crops are grown in rotation with forest re-growth. Other native crops are bullrush millet ( Pennisetum typhoideum ), groundnuts, cassava, sweet potatoes and a variety of curcurbits and legumes. Tobacco is widely cultivated by the natives but mainly on a small scale for private consumption, except in the Petauke District of the Eastern Province where there is a flourishing Burley tobacco industry with a production of 327,000 lb. in 1950. The industry is now organised on co-operative lines. In the tracts adjoining the railway line considerable areas are given over to farming and ranching operations under European management. In the Fort Jameson district there is also a large area taken up by European farmers mainly engaged in tobacco growing. Maize and tobacco are the chief crops of the settler. In the season 1949/50 the total maize on the line of rail was 571,000 bags. The season was favourable for maize growing and there was also an increase in the acreage planted over the previous season. Wheat growing under irrigation has almost disappeared, production in 1950 being only 3,200 bags. Farmers are finding potato, onion and vegetable growing more lucrative than wheat growing. Tobacco growers enjoyed a favourable season. The Eastern area crop reached a figure of 3,214,500 lb., while in the Western area the production was 2,956,000 lb. Turkish tobacco production in the Western tobacco area continued at the low level of the previous year, only 100,000 lb. being produced in 1950. The Headquarters of the Department of Agriculture are at Lusaka. The declared Forest Reserves and Protected Forest Areas now total 2,155 sq. miles. The mining companies cut in 1950 a further 1,281,200 cubic feet of local timber and another 580,000 cubic feet was cut in the territory for minor industries. Other recorded consumption in 1950 included 371,800 poles and 524,000 cords of firewood. The staff of the Forestry Branch of the Department of Agriculture had increased to 16 professional, 21 technical and 5 clerical European officers and a permanent staff of 255 Africans besides casual labour by the end of 1950. Crown land is now granted only on lease, normally for terms of 999 years in the case of farms and 99 years in the case of plots in townships and residential smallholdings, although in special circumstances, leases are granted for shorter periods. As regards agricultural land, preliminary leases are granted in the first instance, the term being 2 years for farms and 3 years for agricultural smallholdings (i.e. areas up to 300 acres). Personal occupation is required by lessees of farms. If the lessee develops his land to the satisfaction of the Crown, on the expiration of his preliminary lease he is granted a lease for 999 years. Crown land suitable for mixed farming, ranching and tobacco-growing is available within easy reach of the railway, though good mixed farming land is now becoming scarce. All farms are unimproved and valuations range from 6s. 8d. to 30s. per acre. Some privately-owned freehold farms are in the market at prices ranging from £2 to £10 or more per acre according to the degree of development. A settler must be in possession of at least £3,000 capital actually available for farming purposes in order to obtain a grant of a leasehold farm from Government. In approved cases, however, New Settlement Loans of up to £1,500 are granted by the Land Board. Applications for Crown land in Northern Rhodesia should be addressed to the Deputy Director of Surveys and Lands, Livingstone. The Department of Mines administers the Mining Ordinance, the Mining and Explosives Regulations, the Mining (Silicosis Prevention) Regulations, the Winding Engine Driver's (Certificate of Competency) Regulations, the Gold Trade Ordinance and the Factories Ordinance and Factories (Safety) Regulations. The Registration of mining titles is carried out by the Registrar of Titles in the Department of Surveys and Lands. The copper produced in the territory in 1950 totalled 276,433 tons, valued at £43,410,364 against 259,081 tons valued at £31,255,168 in 1949. The production of the various copper mines in 1950 was:—Mufulira, 81,317 tons of blister copper; Nchanga, 34,545 tons of blister copper, 10,529 tons of electrolytic copper, and 259 tons of copper concentrates which were sold as concentrates; Nkana, 11,938 tons of blister copper, 67,144 tons of electrolytic copper and 30 tons of copper (other); Roan Antelope, 70,957 tons of blister copper. As a by-product Nkana also produced 34,914 cwtS. of cobalt alloy and 1,296 ozs. of gold, 173,304 ozs. of silver and 27,056 lbs. of selenium, which were recovered from refinery slimes. Lead, zinc and vanadium are produced at Broken Hill, but no vanadium was recovered during 1950. Some of the lead is fabricated at the mine into pipes and sheets for use in the metallurgical plants at Broken Hill and at the copper mines. No iron ore was mined during 1950. Iron ore and manganese ore are used as reagents in the production of lead and zinc. The value of the major products from Broken Hill during 1950 was: lead, £1,537,559, zinc, £3,126,650. Other mineral activities are on a small scale. The mineral production for the years 1949 and 1950 was:— The minerals produced during 1949 were valued at £35,579,543 and those produced during 1950 were valued at £49,323,187. In 1950 the f.o.b. value of total imports was £26,710,475 and the f.o.r. value of total exports £49,941,724. (These figures include Government Stores but exclude Specie.) The Governor. (These notes were prepared by the Federal Government in mid-1951 and consequently do not refer to Sir Henry Gurney's death and to the recent developments. It is intended to include in the 1953 edition a more comprehensive review than has been possible in this first restricted edition. Further information may be obtained from the Annual Report on the Federation of Malaya 1951 which will be published by the Government Press, Kuala Lumpur and, subsequently, by H.M. Stationery Office, London. The Annual Report for 1950 is at present obtainable from H.M. Stationery Office). During 1950, 3,892,000 patients were treated either as out-patients or in-patients in the fixed and travelling dispensaries and welfare clinics in the rural areas. No serious strikes occurred during the period under review, and although there were a number of industrial disputes and some protracted negotiations, industrial peace was generally maintained. This is significant when viewed against its proper background, as many wage adjustments took place as the selling price of tin and rubber increased to no small degree during this period. Unfortunately the cost of living has steadily risen so that the improvement in real wages has not been as substantial as a cursory glance at wage increases would indicate. There was, during 1950, a distinct movement of labour away from the larger plantations to the smaller holdings, where very high wages (but few amenities) obtained, owing to the operation of various sharing systems and as a direct result of slack supervision caused by the absence of many managers and owners. However, towards the end of 1950 and the beginning of 1951, this movement slackened as the smallholdings approached saturation point and as workers began also to appreciate the value of amenities and the better chance of security on the larger plantations. A total of 37,067 man-days were lost during 1950, compared with 5,390½ man-days in 1949. The increase in 1950 was mainly due to demands for wage increases and to differences over the interpretation of certain wage-rate directives in the rubber industry. Mine workers were, on the whole, satisfied with a bonus scheme which increased their earnings as the price of tin improved. After two agreed wage increases had been given in the rubber industry during 1950, further negotiations failed, and the parties decided to go to arbitration. A Board of Arbitration was convened in January, 1951. The figures for the production of tin and rubber during 1950 and the early part of 1951 are in themselves a remarkable tribute to the employers and workers in the Federation, who have been living under the dangerous and insecure conditions of a serious emergency. The "Briggs" plan for regrouping of labour forces has meant that many thousands of workers have had to move their homes and this has thrown a tremendous strain on them and also on their employers. The steadiness and industry of the established labour forces in the larger places of employment have been of inestimable value to the Malayan economy. The technical services of the Department of Social Welfare have been strengthened. Juvenile Courts and an effective Probation Service are in being throughout the Federation of Malaya. As from 1st January, 1951, the functions under the heading of "General Welfare" became the responsibility of the State and Settlement Governments. Social Welfare Officers and their staffs concerned with those functions are now an integral part of the State and Settlement Establishments. The departmental training schemes have been continued and extended with success. The training course for Malayan Probation Officers held in Malacca in 1950 enabled the setting up of the Juvenile Courts and Probation Services. The Central Welfare Council and the State and Settlement Welfare Committees, now independent bodies, together with other voluntary organisations, are taking an increasing part in personal social service and in dealing with special problems as in the campaign against tuberculosis and the building up of blind welfare services. A Malayan Youth Council has been set up. Youth Services have expanded. There has been an increase in the number of rural community centres and village halls. Since the war, a Department of Information has been established. The fundamental purpose of the Information machinery of Government may be defined as being to provide a sustained and intensive effort, with a planned strategy, to raise the level of public understanding and to inculcate in the people interest in, and knowledge of, their political, social and economic responsibilities. In 1951 community radios were installed in some 744 Malayan villages. The Malayan Film Unit has been permanently established and its locally-trained staff are now producing work of considerable value. During 1950, 467 miles of road were re-metalled, hard surface or bitumen sealed and the programme of gradual replacement of old and unserviceable bridges and culverts was continued. The expenditure in 1950 on the maintenance of the 5,373 miles of metalled roads in the Federation amounted to $17,679,000, giving an average cost of $3,290 per mile. The death rate in 1950 was 10.06 per 1,000 and the birth rate, 30.07. There is now only one Government primary school in the Colony in addition to the six Church of England and twenty Roman Catholic aided primary schools and the small private schools. The enrolment in all schools in the Colony in December, 1950, was 2,315 boys and 2,441 girls. Government expenditure on education in 1950 was Rs. 287,933 from local funds and Rs. 101,979 from the Colonial Development and Welfare Fund. Programmes of talks, news and music are now broadcast only on Sundays from ZCQ 3, on a frequency of 5,770 Kc/s. and a wavelength of 52 metres. The Medical Department controls one well-equipped hospital at Victoria, and small, but adequately equipped, hospitals at Anse Royale, Praslin Island and La Digue Island. There is a mental hospital at Anse Royale and a settlement for the treatment of leprosy on Curieuse Island. Dispensaries at four other centres are held weekly. There is also a quarantine station at Long Island and a home for the aged in Victoria. A well-equipped bacteriological laboratory now exists adjacent to Victoria Hospital. Intestinal disease forms the main health problem. There is no malaria, the anopheles mosquito being non-existent in the Seychelles. The estimated expenditure on Medical and Health Services in 1950 was Rs. 421,865. During the year 1950 a sum of Rs. 32,206 was spent on outdoor relief for 854 persons. The average population of the Fiennes Institute was 69 for the year maintained on a cost of Rs. 11,800, excluding salaries. The revision of the poor relief records is now almost complete. (See Note on page 27.) In 1950 the estimated Revenue totalled Rs. 3,976,990 and the estimated Expenditure, Rs. 3,693,751. The Colony's Public Debt amounted to Rs. 390,216. No grants-in-aid of Administration were received during the year but an amount estimated at Rs. 423,148 was received under the Colonial Development and Welfare Acts. The surplus of assets over liabilities on the 31st December, 1950, was Rs. 4,393,898. The General Revenue Stabilization Reserve stood at Rs. 1,592,126. Duty on flour, maize and rice has been abolished. Income Tax is chargeable on all net income remaining after the following deductions unless the chargeable income is less than Rs. 500, in which case no tax is payable :— Earned Income relief $\frac{1}{10}$th of earned income. (Maximum Rs.1,500) Resident's allowance Rs. 500 (or Rs. 2,000 for a married man living with or wholly maintaining his wife) Child allowance .. Rs. 500 per child. (Maximum Rs.2,000) Dependent relative .. Rs. 500. Life insurance .. Amount of premiums. (Maximum $\frac{1}{6}$th of total income) The rates of tax are on a sliding scale commencing at $2\frac{1}{2}$ per cent. on the first Rs. 3,000 of chargeable income and 5 per cent. on chargeable income between Rs. 3,000 and Rs. 6,000. Thereafter the rates of tax increase by $2\frac{1}{2}$ per cent. for each Rs. 1,000 of chargeable income up to a rate of $27\frac{1}{2}$ per cent. for chargeable income between Rs.14,000 and Rs. 15,000. For chargeable income between Rs.15,000 and Rs.25,000 the rate is 30 per cent. and between Rs. 25,000 and Rs. 40,000, 40 per cent. Over Rs. 40,000 the rate is 50 per cent. At the end of 1950 the amount standing to the credit of depositors in the Government Savings Bank was Rs. 3,428,045. (See Note on page 27.) At the end of 1950 the population of the Colony and Protectorate was estimated at 1,975,000. On 7th April, 1951, new Letters Patent issued, to take effect on a date to be notified by proclamation of the Governor, revoking the Letters Patent of 1924. These differ from the earlier Letters Patent principally in that they no longer provide for a Legislative Council and for legislative procedure. The Letters Patent took effect from the 27th November, 1951. New Royal Instructions dated 9th April, 1951, revoke the Instructions of 1924. These provide for an Executive Council of four ex officio members and not less than four unofficial members appointed from among the elected members of the Legislative Council. The Instructions also deal with legislative procedure. On 9th April, 1951, there issued new Orders of the King in Council, one to provide for the Protectorate, the other to provide for the Legislative Council, each revoking the corresponding Orders in Council of 1924. The former Instrument does not differ substantially from the Order in Council of 1924, but the latter provides for a new Legislative Council, to consist of 32 members including the Governor (who is President) and a Vice-President. There are seven ex officio members, seven members elected from the Colony districts, twelve members elected from the Protectorate District Councils, two members elected from the Protectorate Assembly, and two nominated members appointed by the Governor. This Order in Council contains provisions in connection with legislative procedure which in 1924 were included in the Letters Patent. It does not, however, contain the procedure for the election of members to the Legislative Council, as did the Order in Council of 1924. This is now provided for in a local Statute, the Legislative Council (Elections) Ordinance, 1951. In 1951, under the provisions of the District Councils Ordinance, 1950, the basis of representation in the District Councils was broadened by the addition of three members who may be either appointed by the Chiefdom representatives in the District Council or elected for a particular area. By the same Ordinance executive and financial responsibilities are vested in the Councils to enable them to take their full part in the economic development of the Protectorate. Their revenues are derived from Native Administrations, Government and from the Produce Marketing Board. In 1950, 60 youth organisations were registered with the department with an approximate total membership of 5,000. The total revenue and expenditure of the Sierra Leone Government Railway for 1950 was £447,312 and £545,126 respectively. The tonnage of shipping entered and cleared in 1950 was as under :— The landing strips at Bonthe, Kambia and Waterloo are no longer in use. There are now about 2,000 miles of trunk telephone and telegraph lines in the territory and about 2,000 miles of local and urban telephone lines in the Colony and other centres. The trunk system now includes Bo, Mano, Freetown, Njala, Moyamba, Sembehun and Waterloo. On 1st January, 1951, the Post and Telegraphs Department took over responsibility for the telegraph service from the Sierra Leone Government Railway. (See Note on page 27.) In 1950 Revenue amounted to £3,268,530 and Expenditure to £2,978,819. The public debt stood at £3,448,841. The main head of expenditure was £695,616 on Development Schemes. Provincial Administration cost £124,734. £233,959 was spent on Medical Services, £62,539 on Agriculture and £185,559 on Education. The total value of imports in 1950 was £6,745,865 and of exports, £6,959,130. In these figures alloy and nickel specie and currency notes are included at intrinsic value and exports do not include the difference between receipts and payments of the Sierra Leone Produce Marketing Board. The principal import was cotton piece goods valued at £1,579,000. Other important imports were: wearing apparel, machinery, coal, tobacco, flour, and sugar. The largest export was palm kernels valued at £2,278,000; other important exports being diamonds, iron ore, ginger, piassava, cocoa, kola nuts, palm oil, groundnuts and chrome ore. The Islands form a dependency of Jamaica and consist of Grand Cayman, Cayman Brac and Little Cayman. Their total area is about 100 square miles. At the 1943 census the population was 6,670 : Grand Cayman 5,311, Cayman Brac 1,269, Little Cayman 63. Roughly classified according to racial origin 1,052 are of African, 3,518 of mixed and 2,100 of European descent. The principal island, Grand Cayman, is distant about 178 miles from the nearest point of Jamaica. It is about 17 miles in length, with a breadth varying from four to seven miles : very low-lying, it is difficult to sight from the sea. Little Cayman is geographically similar. Cayman Brac is distinguished from the other islands by a massive central limestone bluff. The coasts are for the most part rock-bound, protected by coral reefs enclosing a few fair harbours. Grand Cayman has a magnificent six-mile beach. There are no rivers in any of the islands. The climate from November to March is cool, the prevailing winds being from the north, temperatures range from 60° to 75°. During the summer the range of temperature is some 10° higher and mosquitoes abound. The rainfall at Georgetown during 1951 was 66.54 inches. Hurricanes occur at intervals, as in 1876, 1903, 1909, 1917, 1932 and 1944. The 1932 hurricane was particularly disastrous to Cayman Brac and Little Cayman, resulting in considerable depopulation of the latter. A meteorological station at Georgetown, operated jointly with the Government of Cuba, makes an important contribution to the Caribbean hurricane warning system. The principal towns are Georgetown and West Bay with populations of 1,462 and 1,866 respectively. The islands were discovered by Columbus in 1503. No settlement was made, but they were frequently visited by ships for revictualling purposes. By the Treaty of Madrid in 1670, when Jamaica was ceded to the British Crown, the Cayman Islands were also ceded. The earliest record of a grant of land to settlers is dated 1734. Due to their inaccessibility, the islands were for long a favoured refuge for those seeking to escape from justice. The population in 1774 was 176, in 1802 it had risen to 993, of whom 545 were slaves. In the past century the population has steadily risen. In 1863 the administrative arrangements which had grown up for the internal administration of the Islands were ratified by Act of Parliament. The Government is administered by a Commissioner who is also Judge of the Grand Court. Justices of the Peace (at present 27) are appointed by the Governor of Jamaica, and these, with 27 elected members known as Vestrymen, constitute the Legislative Assembly of Justices and Vestry under the presidency of the Commissioner. The present constitution dates from 1832. All land is individually owned. There is no restriction on alienation to non-natives. A poll tax of 8s. per adult male between the ages of 16 and 60 is collected annually. An ad valorem Customs duty of 15% is levied on most imported commodities, with specific duties on alcoholic liquors and tobacco. There is a preferential tariff on certain Commonwealth goods. The following is a table of public finance in the Dependency. In 1950-51 44% of revenue was derived from Customs receipts. The Public Debt as at 31st March, 1952, was as follows :— Hurricane Loan 1932 : outstanding £1,333. Public Works Loan, 1932 : outstanding £3,143. Reserve Funds at that date totalled £36,417. The Islands have benefited to the extent of £75,907 under the Colonial Development and Welfare Act of 1945. This sum has been devoted to the erection of a new hospital on Grand Cayman, to the improvement of educational facilities, road construction, agricultural education, and health measures mainly directed to the destruction and control of mosquitoes. Excellent results have been achieved in all fields except agriculture, which, in a community persistently addicted to the sea rather than to the land, has made only limited progress. With a view to improvement of communications and the encouragement of the tourist industry, an airfield has been constructed on Grand Cayman financed from the reserve funds of the Dependency, and by a grant from Colonial Development and Welfare funds. During 1952 the Colonial Development Corporation completed and put into operation a cannery on Grand Cayman for processing turtle and manufacturing turtle soup. It is hoped to build up a substantial market in the United States. There is compulsory free elementary education in 13 Government Schools; expenditure in 1950-51 amounted to £6,130 compared with £1,266 in 1936. Attempts are being made to operate three private Secondary Schools in Grand Cayman, but these receive no financial support from Government at present. Expenditure on Medical and Health services during 1952 was £6,026, compared with £479 in 1936. The new hospital will have accommodation for 28 patients. Medical Officers are seconded to the Dependency from Jamaica. A regular three-weekly steamship service is maintained from Kingston to all three islands, and there is a weekly air service between Kingston, Georgetown, and Tampa, Florida, by amphibian aircraft. There is also more or less regular communication with Kingston, Florida and Central American ports by sailing and motor vessels. Motorable roads connect all districts in Grand Cayman and Cayman Brac, but no roads exist in Little Cayman. Wireless stations are maintained by Government at Georgetown, Grand Cayman, and at Stake Bay, Cayman Brac. There are no broadcasting services. The chief industry is catching turtle, which are brought to Grand Cayman before export or conversion to turtle soup. Some of the inhabitants engage in other fishing pursuits and in agriculture, but the mainstay of the economy of the islands is the fact that Caymanians are first-class seamen and readily find employment on the mainland of America, and in United States and Central and South American ships. Their remittances to their families enable a standard of living to be maintained considerably higher than the islands' own resources could justify. There are also some excellent shipwrights, though ship construction is now only sporadic. Trade union legislation is in existence, but no unions have been registered. The islands are not self-supporting in foodstuffs. The production of food crops and raising of cattle are restricted by the character of the limestone soil. The main export product of importance is turtle, the value of which in 1951 was £14,726 compared with £2,865 in 1937. The manufacture of thatch rope is the mainstay of the poorer people and forms a considerable export to Jamaica. The only marketing organization, the Cayman Turtle Association, Limited, was formed in 1951; it works in close association with the Colonial Development Corporation cannery. There are two small co-operative societies at West Bay and Northside. Commissioner and Judge (also Registrar-General) —A. M. Gerrard. The Turks Islands Bank and the Caicos Bank cover an area of approximately 70 by 50 miles. The total land area is estimated at 166 square miles. In 1951 the population was estimated at 6,600 : Turks Islands 2,175, Caicos Islands 4,425. Of the total, 80 were of European, 4,441 of African and 2,079 of mixed descent. Geographically the islands are a part of the Bahama chain and have the same flat characteristics, there being no land above 250 feet. The Turks Islands are separated from the Caicos Islands by a 20-mile stretch of ocean water known as the Turks Islands passage ; the Caicos Islands are surrounded by a shallow water reef. The islands generally are arid and stony. The south-east trade winds blow constantly throughout the year, giving an equable and healthy climate. Rainfall is variable and tends to be higher in the Caicos Islands ; on Grand Turk the annual average is about 21 inches. The mean annual temperature in 1951 was 79.1°. Severe hurricanes occurred in 1866, 1873, 1888, 1926, 1928 and 1945. A United States weather bureau is maintained by Cable and Wireless Limited at Grand Turk. The towns and largest settlement are Grand Turk, Cockburn Harbour (South Caicos) and Bottle Creek (North Caicos), with populations of 1,945, 940 and 1,850 respectively. The Caicos Islands were settled by loyalist planters from the southern States of America after the War of Independence. After the abolition of slavery in 1834 the planters left the islands, their former slaves remaining in possession. In the meantime, in 1799, the islands were annexed by the Bahamas Government, but in 1848 a separate charter was granted to the islands divorcing them from the control of the Bahamas. After a period of severe financial stringency, the Legislative Council in 1873 petitioned Her Majesty that the islands might be annexed to the Colony of Jamaica and from 1874 to the present time they have been one of its dependencies. During the second world war an anti-aircraft battery was stationed at Grand Turk. In March 1952 the United States Government began the construction there of a Guided Missile Observer Station under an extension of the Bahamas Agreement, and a 5,000-feet asphalt airstrip is expected to be completed by 1953. An Order in Council of 1873 which annexed the islands to Jamaica made provision for the constitution of a Legislative Board for the Turks and Caicos Islands. This Board now consists of four official and four unofficial nominated members appointed by the Governor of Jamaica. The Commissioner is ex-officio President of the Board, which has full legislative and budgetary powers, but ordinances require the assent of the Governor of Jamaica before becoming law. Laws passed by the Legislature of Jamaica do not apply to the Dependency unless they are made applicable in express terms. The revenues and expenditure are independent from those of Jamaica. Much of the land on the salt islands is held on private freehold title. The present policy is to limit grants of Crown land to a 99-year lease. The main heads of taxation are Customs import duties, royalty on salt and export duties on crawfish and conchs. The following is a table of public finance in the Dependency :— The estimated ordinary revenue and expenditure for 1952-53 are £40,111 and £47,774 respectively. It is hoped to balance the budget by trading profits on sisal. Up to March 31, 1952, the total assistance approved under the Colonial Development and Welfare Acts, 1940 and 1945, for the Dependency by way of grant amounted to £152,850, plus an interest-free loan of £50,000 towards the cost of taking over the salt industry from its previous owners. The main heads of expenditure from Colonial Development and Welfare Funds up to the end of 1955-56 are estimated to be as follows :— Ninety per cent. of the import trade of the Dependency in 1951-52 was handled by the Competent Authority, a department of the Government. The profits on a turn-over of some £130,000 were almost entirely devoted to food subsidization. All food subsidies ceased on 1st April, 1952, and arrangements to convert the Competent Authority's department into a government-owned trading corporation are well advanced. No further government sponsored development is envisaged, and great difficulty is being experienced in meeting the recurrent costs of the developments effected in recent years. There are 12 Government Elementary Schools and one Senior School in the Dependency. In 1951 the Senior School had an average attendance of 40. There were 1,208 pupils on the rolls of the Elementary Schools and the average attendance was 1,124. Expenditure on education during 1951-52 amounted to £4,000, compared with £840 in 1935. A 14-bed cottage hospital of modern design has just been completed at Grand Turk, together with a dental clinic. 5,000 cases were treated at the dispensary in 1951. Medical officers, seconded from Jamaica, are stationed at Grand Turk and at East Harbour on South Caicos. There is a maternity clinic at Bottle Creek on North Caicos. Total expenditure on medical services in 1951 was £8,000, compared with £990 in 1935. There are airfields at Grand Turk and South Caicos. A weekly air mail service operates through Nassau in the Bahamas. There is a monthly mail, freight and passenger service to Jamaica by sea and ships call occasionally from England and the United States. Grand Turk is an important junction of cables to Jamaica, Barbados and Bermuda. A radio elephone system connects Grand Turk with Salt Cay and the Caicos as well as providing a service with Jamaica and the United States. Most of the population of the Turks Islands group and of South Caicos are engaged in the production (by solar evaporation) and shipping of salt. The remainder of the Caicos population are mainly fishermen, and do a limited amount of subsistence agriculture. A large proportion of the male population is usually absent from home working in the Bahamas lumber camps, the Inagua salt works or as crews on merchant ships. Sisal is grown under conditions of peasant agriculture in the Caicos Islands, but the quality is poor, owing to the absence of fresh water for retting and washing. During 1951 about 60 tons were exported. Salt is the main product of the Dependency and the output over the past 25 years has averaged about 43,000 tons annually. Crawfish are exported frozen to Florida, and conchs are sold dried in Haiti. Imports Exports In 1938, 8.1% of total imports came from the United Kingdom, 21.8% from other Commonwealth countries, and 70.1% from foreign countries. The corresponding percentages in 1951 were 21.5%, 33.5% and 45.0%. In 1938, 6.4% of total exports went to the United Kingdom, 57.7% to other Commonwealth countries, and 35.9% to foreign countries. The corresponding percentages in 1951 were: Nil, 37.8% and 62.2%. The Grand Turk Credit Union, Limited, incorporated in 1947, has 80 members and share capital of £1,000.
